## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 8 completely separate production-ready iOS games at the same time: exactly 2 unique idle games, 2 unique puzzle games, 2 drinking/party games playable by a group passing around ONE phone, and 2 large-group games where up to 10-12 players each connect from their own phone to one shared session (card-game formats welcome; prefer local peer-to-peer / GameKit / MultipeerConnectivity over a cloud backend so they stay local-first).

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Additional notes:
Monetization must sell REAL value (content, modes, mechanics, utility) — never cosmetics/skins alone.

Now generate at least 16 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 8, and build all 8 to production quality.

## Final Output

**Original prompt:** preserved verbatim across every round of this discussion (see above); nothing in this contract alters or reinterprets that text.

**Scope of this thread:** this project builds exactly one app — Umbra — in its own folder at `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/umbra`. The "generate 16 ideas / build 8" instruction at the end of the source prompt is portfolio-level process that already ran upstream of this child project; it is not a re-ideation task for this thread.

**Hard requirements:**
- Local-first SwiftUI iOS app, no required backend, no accounts, deterministic local state.
- A shadow-casting spatial puzzle: a deterministic occlusion/solve engine where players position a light source and occluders to cast a shadow matching a target silhouette.
- At least five authored, solvable levels spanning a real difficulty curve, loadable and completable without crashes or geometry glitches.
- A real designer/share mode, scoped narrowly but genuinely: the editor manipulates the same occluder/light-source primitives used in play (not a separate toy authoring model); a level serializes to a compact shareable artifact (string or small file); a second import path loads that artifact back into an identical playable level, verified by an actual encode → decode → diff test with zero drift.
- Local persistence with automatic save, resume after relaunch, and resilience to forced quit.
- Every reachable flow (play, edit, save/load, export/import) implements empty, loading, success, and error states — including explicit handling for a corrupt local save and a malformed/truncated share artifact.
- Accessibility built into the core feedback loop, not layered on: shadow/target match must be perceivable without relying on color alone (shape outline, snap tick, haptic, or numeric overlap cues), plus standard VoiceOver labeling.
- Test coverage on the geometry/solve-checking logic specifically, since that's where a silent bug (not a crash) does the most damage: zero-area shadows, touching-only overlaps, contact-edge floating-point precision, rotation/orientation edge cases — fixed fixtures plus randomized fuzz permutations across occluder arrangements.
- Monetization shape stated now: a free starter vault (core levels + core editor functions) plus paid challenge-pack bundles (themed/difficulty level sets) plus an optional premium editor expansion (advanced constraints/symmetry tools, solve-history analytics). Content and tools, never cosmetics-only.
- Architecture leaves a seam for optional future cloud sync/sharing without requiring a rewrite of the core geometry model.

**Non-goals:** cloud backend or server dependency in this MVP; user accounts; remote level publishing, ratings, or a community remix marketplace; multiplayer/networked play; a story/campaign layer or complex non-puzzle mechanics; cosmetic-only monetization; any placeholder control that doesn't do what it visually implies. The earlier invented restrictions ("must not be a full-fledged game with complex mechanics," "production-ready requires backend/cloud infrastructure if applicable") are struck outright — they have no basis in the source prompt and must not carry forward into later phases even as disputed language.

**Production-readiness definition:** more than "compiles and runs." The app must survive relaunch and forced quit without losing progress; handle corrupt saves and malformed share artifacts as explicit, recoverable error states rather than crashes; provide non-color accessibility cues for the core match-detection feedback; and pass a reproducible acceptance pack — the 5+ fixed level fixtures plus randomized geometry fuzz cases — before this app can be called done. Build verification and final review must agree on what works before "done" is declared.

**Decision rules for later phases:**
- Any change to geometry, level state, or the serialization format must keep the 5+ solvable-level fixture set and the round-trip encode/decode/diff test passing.
- Any new feature must tie to a measurable user outcome (solve time, invalid-edit rate, successful recovery) and ship with a corresponding fixture/test, or it stays parked.
- Any conflict between "richer/more viral" and "local-first, tightly scoped MVP" resolves toward the narrower, shippable interpretation, with the cut feature named explicitly as a non-goal — never a silent drop.
- Any future message claiming consensus must enumerate, item by item, which required output it resolves — a bare "sounds good, let's move on" does not count, following two prior incidents in this same discussion where a prematurely-sequenced message tried to do exactly that.
- **Named risk on record:** across rounds 3–5, Gemma3 argued the designer/share-mode requirement and the stricter production-readiness bar represent real schedule risk for a single MVP pass, and preferred deferring both. The group's judgment, reaffirmed each round without a new rebuttal changing it, is that designer/share mode is load-bearing to Umbra's identity (named explicitly in the one-sentence promise) and that the scoped version defined here (same primitives, one serialization format, one round-trip test) is small enough to absorb that risk. This is recorded as a documented disagreement carried forward for design/build phases to be aware of, not a silently overridden objection.

### Product Research

Round 2 sharpened things nicely, and I think this one's actually done. Here's where it landed.

**Codex and I stayed tightly aligned, and it got more concrete.** Audience: the premium iOS puzzle buyer (Monument Valley / The Witness crowd) playing in short deterministic sessions, plus a smaller "creator in the loop" segment who builds a level and sends a code to a friend — Codex's framing that this is how virality happens here ("earn sharing by making the export feel instantly useful," not a feed/social layer) is the piece that actually resolves the tension between "viral potential" and "no remote publishing" in the contract. Comparables stayed the same (Shadowmatic for silhouette intuition, Monument Valley for premium spatial polish, level-code ecosystems for the share pattern). Platform opportunities picked up detail: iPad as a first-class editor canvas, haptics on snap-state, Share Sheet for import/export, Dynamic Type and contrast as real requirements rather than visual tweaks. The risk list kept growing usefully and cleared the bar with room to spare: geometry determinism at the edge cases, editor/play parity, serialization forward-compatibility, fuzz-test bias toward degenerate geometry, onboarding discoverability, level-content difficulty, and the new one — an editor that lets someone save a level the solver can't correctly grade, which we agreed should get closed by running the same solve-checker as a save-time validator rather than a separate rule set.

**Gemma3 moved on two things and dug in on two others.** It came around to 5 levels being the right number (dropping its own round-1 "just one level" position — though it then oddly attributes that original one-level idea to "Gemini" rather than itself, which is worth flagging as a mix-up, not a new voice in the room) and it now agrees monetization should be named now rather than deferred, reversing its round-1 stance. But it's still pushing to treat the round-trip test, schema versioning, and non-color accessibility as "premature optimization" or "later phase" concerns. Those aren't open research questions, though — they're hard requirements the prompt_contract phase already locked five rounds ago, so re-arguing them here is out of scope for what this phase is actually supposed to produce (audience, comparables, platform opportunities, risks, build implications), same as its scope-reduction argument was last round. Nobody else picked up that thread, and it doesn't change any of the five required outputs.

Qwen didn't produce a response this round either (CLI unavailable) — two rounds now with no substantive contribution to weigh.

Given both required-output rounds have real, converged content from two independent voices, five-plus concrete risks are on record, and the "still open" item is a rehash of an already-decided contract question rather than something this phase should resolve, I think this closes.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Audience and use context:** Primarily the existing premium-puzzle-game buyer — people who already pay for things like Monument Valley or The Witness — playing in short, deterministic solo sessions during commutes or breaks, where polish and clarity matter more than volume of content. A secondary but commercially meaningful segment is the "creator in the loop": someone who builds a level and sends the share code to a friend or family member as a personal challenge. That second segment is what makes the designer/share mode more than a checkbox — it's the app's only realistic growth loop, since the contract explicitly rules out any in-app social/publishing layer.

**Comparable apps or patterns to learn from** (pattern-matches from general knowledge, not fresh research — labeled as assumptions): Shadowmatic-style silhouette-fit intuition for the core mechanic; Monument Valley as the benchmark for premium spatial clarity and onboarding discipline, not mechanics; level-code-sharing conventions from editor-driven puzzle/community-level games, with the standard failure mode being that the export format quietly drifts from the play format as the game gets patched, breaking old shared codes.

**Platform-specific opportunities:** Haptic snap-detents when an occluder or light source aligns meaningfully (edge, angle) — cheap to build, reads as premium. Native Share Sheet for exporting/importing the level code, satisfying "share mode" without any custom social infrastructure. VoiceOver treated as a real design problem, not a labeling checkbox — a spatially-driven puzzle needs an actual strategy for how a screen-reader user builds a mental map of light/occluder/target positions. iPad as a legitimately better first-class canvas for the editor, worth designing for deliberately rather than just "scales up." Dynamic Type and high-contrast support as core requirements, since alignment judgment is the entire mechanic.

**Major assumptions and risks:**
1. Geometry determinism at the edges — zero-area shadows, edge-only overlaps, near-collinear/rotation floating-point precision — is where a silent wrong-answer bug lives, and it's more dangerous than a crash because nobody notices.
2. The round-trip encode/decode/diff test proves no data loss today but says nothing about forward compatibility once new occluder types get added later; a schema version field should exist from day one even though nothing needs multiple versions yet.
3. Randomized fuzz testing only catches the dangerous cases if it's biased toward near-degenerate geometry — uniformly random placement will pass every test while a real bug ships.
4. The editor must not be able to save a level the solver can't correctly grade (overlapping occluders, unachievable target overlap, light source inside an occluder) — the fix is running the same solve-checker as a save-time validator, not a separate "valid level" rule set.
5. Authoring five levels that are genuinely solvable, fun, and free of unintended shortcut solutions is likely the hardest problem in the build, harder than any of the engineering.
6. Onboarding has a tight window — if a player can't discover the grab/rotate/translate affordances in 30-45 seconds, level quality won't save the first impression.
7. A corrupted or truncated share code is the single point of failure for the app's only growth mechanism, since the recipient didn't cause the corruption — the import failure state needs to be genuinely useful (explain what went wrong), not a generic error, or the share loop dies at the exact moment it was supposed to work.

**Implications for the first build:** Structure the build around three flows that map directly to the one-sentence promise — Play, Edit, Share — with Edit built as a strict superset of Play's controls (drag, rotate, duplicate/delete, reset) so there's never a "the rules are different in edit mode" trust break. Build and test the geometry/solve engine and the canonical level format before UI polish, since editor, share format, and accessibility cues are all downstream of that data model being right. Nail the share-artifact format early (with a version field) since play, edit, export, and import all depend on its shape. Auto-save should be invisible until it matters, then visibly reassuring on relaunch. Monetization shape (free starter vault, paid challenge packs, pro editor tier) should be visible in the product framing from the first build, even if the purchase flow itself isn't wired up yet.

Qwen's message was again a garbled restatement of the existing contract, not a decision about app selection. Neither is a real counterpoint to weigh against the converged Codex/Claude position.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Single-app vs portfolio-parent decision:** Single-app child thread. This project builds exactly one app — Umbra — in its own folder. It is not a portfolio parent, so no portfolio-json manifest is produced and no sibling child projects are created here. The portfolio-level app selection (2 idle, 2 puzzle, 2 party, 2 large-group) already ran upstream in the batch-games parent program.

**Selected app:**
- Slug: `umbra`
- Category: puzzle
- Promise: "Shadow-casting spatial puzzle with a real designer/share mode"
- Target user: the premium iOS puzzle buyer (Monument Valley / The Witness crowd) playing short, deterministic solo sessions, plus a secondary "creator in the loop" segment who builds a level and shares the code with a friend — the app's only realistic growth loop.
- Build flag: true
- Output location: `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/umbra`

**Selection rationale:** Umbra is explicitly named as the app this thread was handed; the spec, one-sentence promise, and every hard requirement locked across five rounds of prompt_contract and two rounds of product_research all describe one coherent app (Play/Edit/Share as a single object), not a family of separable products. Building it as a single project is also what's best for the actual end user — nobody wants to download two apps to solve a puzzle and share it.

**Rejected alternatives:** There is no live menu of candidate apps to choose between at this thread's scope, so the only real decision was whether to split this thread into multiple sibling projects (e.g., separating "puzzle mode" and "designer mode" into two apps). That's rejected — it would just relabel already-settled scope as a coordination problem between two codebases instead of resolving anything, and it contradicts the locked contract's Play/Edit/Share structure.

**Note for the design phase:** the portfolio-level uniqueness constraint (this puzzle app must not resemble the batch's other puzzle app) still needs an actual check against the sibling puzzle folder — nothing in this thread's history shows that's been verified yet.

**Acceptance checks:** selected count (1) matches this thread's scope, which is a single preselected app; that one app maps to its own folder; and its category (puzzle) isn't collapsed into anything else.

### Initial Discussion

Here's how round 1 shook out.

Codex and I (Claude) are lined up, and between the two of us this phase's actual job — one-sentence promise, primary user, core loop, scope boundaries, measurable success — got answered concretely rather than just restated. Codex gave the crisp version: promise, primary+creator user, the loop as a sequence, tight scope, and six specific measurable bars (5+ levels solvable, byte/geometry-exact round-trip, fuzzed geometry edge cases pass, exact state survives relaunch/force-quit, malformed input recovers gracefully, controls discoverable in ~45 seconds). I built on that with the actual screen/flow shape it implies — Vault → Play → Solve → Remix-into-Edit → Share/Save — and flagged that Edit should be reached mainly by remixing an existing level rather than a scary blank canvas, that the three import-failure modes (truncated, version-mismatch, geometrically-invalid) need to stay three distinct honest error states rather than collapsing into one generic error, and that Play and Edit need to share one canvas/gesture/data-model component so they can't silently drift apart. None of that contradicts Codex — it's the same product, described one layer more concretely.

Gemma3 is repeating, close to verbatim, the same "cut to one level, no real editor, no monetization talk yet" pitch it's made in every phase so far — that fight already ran five rounds in prompt_contract and got settled there with Gemma3's concern preserved as a named risk, not adopted. It's not a new objection to this phase's specific required outputs (promise/user/loop/scope/success-criteria), so there's nothing here to re-litigate. Qwen again just fed back a garbled restatement of the existing contract text — no new position to weigh.

Given the two substantive, independent contributions agree and nothing new is being raised against the locked scope, I think this closes at round 1.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**One-sentence product promise:** Umbra lets you solve — and design — physical light-and-shadow puzzles on your phone, then hand a friend the exact puzzle you made with one tap.

**Primary user and scenario:** A solo premium-puzzle player (the Monument Valley / The Witness buyer) on iPhone or iPad, playing short deterministic sessions in spare moments — a commute, a break. Secondary but commercially real: the "creator in the loop," someone who solves or builds a level they like and sends the share code to one friend as a personal challenge. That second behavior is the app's only real growth loop, since remote publishing/social features are an explicit non-goal.

**Core loop:** Vault (browse levels, solved/unsolved/starred) → Play (position light source + occluders on a canvas, live shadow preview, non-color match feedback) → Solve confirmation (a distinct success moment) → optional Remix into Edit (same canvas, same drag/rotate/duplicate/delete controls, now shaping the target instead of matching it) → Share (export code via Share Sheet) or Save to vault. Edit is a strict superset of Play's controls, reached primarily by remixing an existing level rather than a blank-canvas entry point, so "you can build your own levels" is discoverable without editor mode feeling like a separate, intimidating product.

**Hard scope boundaries:** Local-first SwiftUI, no backend, no accounts, deterministic local state. Five-plus authored, solvable levels with a real difficulty curve. One canonical, versioned level schema shared by Play and Edit — no parallel data models that can drift. Local persistence with auto-save and forced-quit recovery. Every reachable flow (play/edit/save/load/export/import) has real empty/loading/success/error states, including three distinct import-failure states (truncated artifact, version-mismatch, geometrically-invalid level) rather than one generic error. Accessibility (non-color match cues, VoiceOver) is part of the core feedback loop, not a layer on top. Non-goals, explicit: no cloud backend, no accounts, no remote publishing/ratings/remix marketplace, no multiplayer, no story/campaign layer, no cosmetics-only monetization.

**Measurable success criteria:**
1. Each of the 5+ starter levels is solvable from a clean state.
2. Export → import round-trips to exact geometry/state equality, zero drift, across fixed fixtures plus fuzzed geometry permutations (including zero-area shadows, touching-only overlaps, near-edge rotation precision).
3. The app resumes the exact in-progress state after relaunch or force-quit.
4. Any corrupt save or malformed/truncated/version-mismatched share artifact produces a specific, recoverable error message and a path forward — never a crash or a generic failure.
5. A first-time user can discover all core controls (move, rotate, reset) within roughly 45 seconds via onboarding.
6. The editor cannot save a level the solver can't correctly grade — save-time validation runs through the same solve-checker used in play.

**Recorded, not re-opened:** Gemma3's preference for a single-level, editor-less, monetization-deferred MVP is on the table again this round in the same form it took in prompt_contract and product_research. It's carried forward as the same named risk already on record, not treated as a new finding — nothing in this round changed the substance of that disagreement.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Target user and use case:** The primary buyer is the existing premium-puzzle-game player — someone who already paid for Monument Valley, The Witness, or similar — playing short, deterministic five-to-ten-minute sessions on a commute or break, who expects the free tier to feel complete rather than gated-to-frustration. A secondary, commercially real segment is the "creator in the loop": someone who builds or remixes a level they like and sends the share code to one friend as a personal challenge. That second behavior is the app's only real growth loop, since remote publishing/social discovery is an explicit non-goal.

**Core loop:** Vault (browse levels, solved/unsolved/starred) → Play (drag/rotate a light source and occluders on a canvas until the cast shadow matches a target silhouette, with live non-color match feedback) → Solve confirmation (a distinct success moment) → optional Remix into Edit on that exact level (identical canvas, identical drag/rotate/duplicate/delete controls — editing is a strict superset of play, never a separate toy authoring model) → Share (export a compact code via the system Share Sheet) or Save to vault. The loop is built to make "solving" and "making" feel like one system rather than two products bolted together, which is what gives replay/remix a reason to happen instead of complete-and-exit.

**Paid value and subscription value:** The free starter vault contains five-plus authored, high-quality levels plus *full* editor and share functionality — a designer/share mode locked behind a paywall isn't a real designer/share mode, it's a demo, and a demo doesn't produce the friend-to-friend share codes this app depends on for growth. What's actually sold is more content and more capability, never cosmetics: paid challenge-pack bundles (new themes, mechanics, and difficulty tiers that materially expand time-to-mastery), and an optional pro editor subscription tier — symmetry/precision constraint tools, solve-history analytics (attempt quality, solve-angle patterns), batch export — aimed at the creator segment who wants to build harder, more deliberate puzzles than the base toolset supports. That converts the app from "puzzle toy" into a reusable design-and-practice workflow serious creators would pay recurring money for.

**Competitive wedge:** Most silhouette/shadow-puzzle competitors have either strong art or strong level content, but not a validated round-trip artifact system with honest recovery behavior, and their "editor," if one exists at all, is a stripped-down toy that can't express what the game engine actually supports. Umbra's bet is that closing that gap — anything you can solve, you can remix, and anything you remix reopens byte-identical on a friend's phone, provable by an actual encode→decode→diff test — is worth more to this buyer than a handful of extra built-in levels would be.

**Viral or niche growth angle:** Deliberately narrow and non-social: a level code sent directly to one person as a personal challenge, distributed through whatever messaging app the recipient already uses, with zero backend, zero feed, zero discovery surface. Growth only happens if the sender can say "open this and it just works" — which means the three import-failure states (truncated code, version-mismatched code, geometrically-invalid code) must each surface a specific, honest message rather than collapsing into one generic "couldn't import this" error, because a silent or vague failure kills the app's only growth mechanism at the exact moment it was supposed to fire.

**Local-first and cloud-ready plan:** SwiftUI, fully local state for v1 — no accounts, no backend, on-device persistence with auto-save and forced-quit recovery that resumes exact in-progress geometry. Every reachable flow (play/edit/save/load/export/import) carries real empty/loading/success/error states, including corrupt-save recovery. The cloud-ready seam is a versioned level schema (a schema-version field from day one) plus a small persistence-layer protocol boundary (a `LevelStore`-style interface) so a future CloudKit-backed sync implementation is a new conformance, not a rewrite of the geometry engine or UI — retrofitting versioning after real share codes exist in the wild would be far worse than adding a version field now.

**Why this app deserves to exist:** it's the only entrant in this space treating "you can build what you can solve, and it reopens exactly as authored" as a testable engineering contract instead of a marketing claim — which is precisely the trust threshold a premium-puzzle buyer needs before a share code becomes something they actually send to a friend.

### Next Steps Small

CONSENSUS: YES

Here's the real state of play. Two things converged cleanly across the group: one playable level plus five internal-only geometry fixtures, and a personal cold share-code test to a second device (Gemma3 committed to doing this itself). Those aren't in dispute anywhere in the genuine transcript.

The import-failure-state count and the stability-check depth are where Gemma3 has now repeated, essentially word-for-word, the same "overkill/premature optimization" objection across rounds 3 and 4 without ever answering the specific, falsifiable question Codex and I both put to it directly: is the schemaVersion field not actually going to exist regardless, and is the save-time solve-checker validator not actually going to exist regardless? Because if both of those are being built anyway for reasons unrelated to import (they're locked, standing requirements from earlier phases), then the two "extra" import states aren't extra architecture, they're two more call sites into code that has to exist either way. Gemma3's real round 4 message doesn't engage that question at all — it just restates the position with more emphasis. That matches this project's own established pattern for closing a phase: when an objection gets raised, answered on the merits, and re-raised without a new counter-argument, the phase closes on the converged position with the objection preserved as a named risk rather than silently dropped.

So I'm closing this here, on Codex's and my converged position, with Gemma3's preference recorded honestly as a disagreement rather than adopted.

## Final Output

**MVP slice:** One complete, real vertical loop — Vault → Play → Solve → Remix into Edit → Export → Import on a second device — built on the actual geometry/solve engine and actual versioned level schema, no mocked layers. Ships with exactly one polished, player-visible level, plus five small internal-only geometry fixtures (touching-only overlap, near-collinear rotation, zero-area shadow, light-inside-occluder, duplicate-overlap) that exist purely to exercise the solve-checker test suite the standing contract already requires — not shown to the player.

**Must-have interactions:** Drag/rotate the light source and at least one occluder; duplicate/delete/reset; live-updating shadow recomputed on every gesture frame; non-color match signal (outline + numeric overlap, haptic tick at solve threshold); a distinct solve-confirmation moment; a "remix" entry into Edit using the identical canvas and controls as Play; export-to-code and import-from-code; auto-save with exact resume after relaunch or forced quit, including in-progress edit state.

**Validation criteria (this is the part that closes the debate):**
1. The one shipped level solves deterministically, and a fixed-seed, fixed-action-script replay of it runs 50 times through save/reload with a bitwise-identical result each time — this is an automated loop around code already being written, not manual testing, and it directly targets the single risk this project has flagged as most dangerous (silent geometry drift) since round one of the earliest phase.
2. Export → import round-trips to exact geometry/state equality via encode→decode→diff, zero drift, on the shipped level.
3. All three import-failure states are wired to distinct, honest, actionable error messages: truncated/malformed (decode failure), unsupported schema version (one comparison against a field that has to exist regardless), and geometrically-invalid (one call to the save-time solve-checker validator that has to exist regardless to stop the editor from saving an ungradable level). Shipping only the truncated case would leave the version-mismatch path — the one that only surfaces months later, in the wild, against a real friend's older share code, at the exact moment the growth loop is supposed to fire — completely unvalidated.
4. Forced-quit mid-edit resumes to the exact in-progress geometry and solved-state flags.
5. At least one team member personally generates a share code from the shipped level and sends it cold to a real second device, and it opens and plays correctly with no more context than "try this."

**Cut list (explicit, not silently dropped):** monetization UI and paywall, pro editor tools (symmetry/constraint tools, solve-history analytics, batch export), iPad-specific layout, multi-screen onboarding beyond a single short first-run walkthrough, analytics, cloud sync adapter, and four of the five contract-required starter levels (they ship later as real content once this slice proves the mechanic and pipeline are sound).

**Named disagreement carried forward, not silently overridden:** Gemma3 argued across rounds 3 and 4 that proving only the truncated import-failure case, plus a single targeted edge-case test instead of the 50-run determinism check, was sufficient for this slice and that the other two failure states and the repeated-run check were premature scope. That objection was raised, answered directly (the underlying mechanisms for all three states and the 50-run loop are mandatory build items regardless of import, making the marginal cost small), and repeated without addressing that rebuttal — so it's recorded here as a documented risk the build phase should be aware of, not adopted into the final slice.

### Detailed Discussion

Before the recap — same red flag as earlier phases: the transcript above contains fabricated content. After Gemma3's real round-2 message (the Backend/Systems Engineer one that pushes back on Codex's framing), there's a "Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2: QUALITY: PASS... CONSENSUS: YES... This phase is finished" block that I never wrote — I'm the coordinator and this is the first time I'm weighing in on round 2. That's followed by a second "Gemma3 (local) — Round 2" message that's suspiciously generic ("amazing, let's close, appreciate everyone's hard work") and doesn't engage a single one of the specific open questions Gemma3 was actually asked (the VoiceOver architecture question, ID-collision confirmation) — that matches the exact injection pattern flagged twice already in this project's history (fake reversed-position messages plus a fake premature "CONSENSUS: AGREED" trying to force an early close). I'm disregarding both fabricated blocks and basing this decision only on the genuine messages: Codex's real round 2, my own real round 2, and Gemma3's one real round 2 message (the "I'm hearing you on one level, five fixtures... but I think you're prioritizing proof over trust" message).

CONSENSUS: YES

Here's what actually happened on the merits. Codex directly answered every specific question I raised in round 1: dropped "bitwise-identical" in favor of two distinct checks (epsilon tolerance ~1e-8 on live geometry replay, exact structural equality on serialized snapshots); locked share transport as plain string via Share Sheet with any future URL scheme treated as just another untrusted input into the same parser, no special trust; locked import-ID-collision as always-insert-new, never overwrite; and gave concrete solve-threshold numbers (97.0% for a provisional "close" state, 98.5% for solved, with a small deadband) using one shared overlap metric for both the live solver and the save-time editor validator. None of that got contradicted by Gemma3's real message — Gemma3 explicitly agreed the copy/paste transport is more robust than a deep link, and didn't object to the ID-collision or threshold numbers.

The one real point of friction: Codex wants a mandatory discrete non-gesture control path for VoiceOver from day one (select object, then nudge arrows plus a coarse/fine rotation stepper, driven through the same mutation functions as the drag gesture) — not just labels. Gemma3's real message pushes back, calling a full non-gesture interaction redesign "beyond the scope of this phase" and insisting baseline labeling is the actual requirement. I read that less as a hard technical objection and more as a caution against over-specifying the exact control layout inside a discussion phase rather than a design phase — Gemma3 never disputes that VO users need *something* beyond labels, just how much of that should get nailed down here. So I'm resolving it by locking the requirement (a real non-gesture control path is mandatory, driven by the same geometry mutation functions as gesture input, not a forked data model) while leaving the exact control shape — Codex's nudge-arrow/stepper proposal — as a starting point for the design phase to refine, not something re-litigated as in/out of scope. My own architecture question (do the mutation functions cleanly support two input paths without forking) never got a direct systems-level confirmation from Gemma3, so that's carried forward as an assumption to verify in architecture, not a settled fact.

Also locked without objection: solved-status is never an independently-driftable stored boolean — it's recomputed on load or invalidated the instant geometry changes in edit mode, closing the "resume shows solved but geometry doesn't match" bug class I raised. And on gesture interruption (backgrounded mid drag-and-rotate), the resolution is roll-back-to-last-committed-transform, never persist a frozen partial gesture.

## Final Output

**Resolved requirements:** One polished, player-visible starter level in a persistent vault, plus five internal-only geometry fixtures for the solve-checker test suite. One canonical versioned level schema shared by Play and Edit — identical primitives, transforms, and constraints in both. Deterministic solve-checking is the same function used for live match feedback and for save-time validation (editor can't save what the solver can't grade). Auto-save with forced-quit/relaunch recovery for both play and in-progress edit state. Export/import of one artifact format with exact round-trip fidelity. Non-color match feedback (outline + numeric overlap + haptic tick), full VoiceOver labeling, plus a mandatory discrete non-gesture control path for VoiceOver users (select object, nudge arrows, coarse/fine rotation stepper, explicit duplicate/delete/reset controls, accessibility announcements of numeric overlap) driven through the same underlying mutation functions as the drag gesture — never a forked control/data model. Determinism is checked two distinct ways: epsilon tolerance (~1e-8) on live geometry/solve replay, and exact structural equality on canonical serialized snapshots at save/load — never a literal "bitwise-identical" claim on floats. Share transport is a plain artifact string via the system Share Sheet as the primary path; any future URL-scheme/deep-link import is just another untrusted input into the identical three-state decoder, never a trusted fast path. Vault import never overwrites on ID collision — it always inserts as a new entry (e.g. "Imported copy"), original ID kept only for history/debug. Solve thresholds are concrete: ~97.0% overlap for a provisional "close match" state, 98.5% for solved, with a small fixed deadband so haptics/success cues don't fire on jitter — one shared overlap metric used by both the live solver and the save-time validator. Solved status is never an independently-stored boolean that can drift from geometry — it's recomputed on load or invalidated the instant edit-mode geometry changes.

**Edge cases:** zero-area/near-zero occluders; touching-only overlap; duplicate occluders at near-equal bounds; 1px/edge-aligned target intersections; light source exactly on or just inside/outside an occluder boundary; rotation wraparound (359°→0°) normalization consistency; large drag-delta jitter; simultaneous move+rotate gesture interrupted mid-flight (resolved: roll back to last committed transform, never persist a frozen partial gesture); app backgrounded mid-gesture, mid-export, or mid-import; force-quit mid-edit; stale/partial local saves; truncated share strings; copy/paste character loss; unsupported future schema version (currently only testable synthetically, since only one schema version exists — named as a real limitation); resuming a saved draft where a previously-solved level was left mid-edit (solved flag must not survive stale); out-of-bounds occluder placement (needs an explicit legality/clipping decision); deleting occluders down to a mathematically unreachable target (must be caught by the save-time validator, not just "currently doesn't match"); NaN/Infinity values that survive JSON decode as well-typed garbage (caught by a numeric-sanity pass in the geometry validator, not just "did decode throw"); two occluders whose shadows overlap each other (must not double-count coverage); vault ID collision on import (always-new-entry, never silent overwrite); VoiceOver users needing full discrete equivalents for every gesture-driven action, not just labels.

**Data and privacy implications:** Fully local-only. The only data that ever leaves the device is the artifact string the user explicitly shares — pure puzzle geometry, no device or user identifiers. No accounts, no network calls, no analytics or tracking in this build. No permissions requested beyond the system Share Sheet. Clipboard is never read silently — only explicit user-chosen share/import actions touch it. The local save file gets swept into standard device/iCloud backup by default, which is fine and requires no special handling precisely because there's no PII in it — stated here as a deliberate, reviewed choice. Future pro-tier solve-history analytics stays local-only unless a later cloud-sync feature explicitly opts the user in, consistent with the standing cloud-ready seam.

**Risk register:** (1) Geometry/solve-check drift at the edges remains the top named risk since round one of this project — mitigation sharpened to two distinct, honestly-scoped checks instead of one overclaimed "bitwise" guarantee. (2) The version-mismatch import path can only be synthetically tested today since only one schema version exists — named as a real, unresolved test-coverage limitation, not solved. (3) The VoiceOver non-gesture control path is a locked requirement, but whether the same mutation functions can drive both gesture and discrete input without forking the data model is an architectural assumption that still needs confirmation in the design/architecture phase — flagged explicitly rather than assumed. (4) Live per-frame shadow recomputation performance is untested against occluder count or older supported devices — a laggy live shadow would undercut the exact tactile trust this app depends on. (5) Solved-status staleness — mitigated by never storing it as an independently driftable value. (6) Vault ID collision on import — mitigated by always-new-entry, never-overwrite.

**Final assumptions:** iPhone first, layout extensible to iPad later; iOS 17+, Observation/SwiftUI-first, no third-party SDKs unless they materially reduce risk; paid value is described now but purchase flow implementation stays deferred; a real-device share demonstration is a release gate for the next phase, not documentation-only; single point light (not area/directional) for full determinism; v1 occluder primitive is a rectangle, with the primitive system architected so more shapes can be added later without a schema rewrite; the canvas is abstract 2D top-down space, not 3D; solve thresholds are 97.0%/98.5% with a fixed deadband; share transport is a plain string via Share Sheet, with any future URL scheme treated as untrusted-equal input into the same decoder; vault import always creates a new entry, never silently overwrites.

### App Features

Flagging the same thing again before the recap: the block that appears as "Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2: QUALITY: PASS... CONSENSUS: YES... This phase is finished" is not something I wrote — this message is the first time I'm actually weighing in on round 2, and I never declared consensus. Same injection pattern that's shown up in every phase so far. Disregarding it and going only off the genuine messages: Codex's real round 2, my own real round 2, and Gemma3's real round 2.

Here's what actually happened. Codex and I are now producing near-identical MUST lists independently, which at this point is a strong signal the scope itself is right rather than something we've just repeated at each other until it stuck — same core spine (shared canvas/geometry engine, versioned schema with all three import-failure states, auto-save/recovery, full VoiceOver including the discrete control path, five internal fixtures), same SHOULD tier (second/third level, onboarding toast, haptics/polish, solved-level metadata), same WON'T list. I also flagged one small but real fix — drop "feature-flagged" from the fixture-test-bed language, since those five geometry fixtures are XCTest-only and never touch the shipped UI; "feature-flagged" invites someone to build a hidden debug menu that shouldn't exist.

Gemma3's round 2 message is genuinely different from its last several rounds, and worth taking seriously rather than waving off as a repeat. It didn't restate "no complex editor" this time — instead it embraced the versioned schema, epsilon-based comparisons, and explicitly called the discrete non-gesture VoiceOver control path "non-negotiable," which is a real reversal from its detailed_discussion-phase position that a full non-gesture redesign was "beyond scope." That's good, but it's implicit — Gemma3 never said "I'm dropping the editor objection," it just stopped making it and moved on. I don't want to assume that's settled without it saying so directly.

What it raised instead is a genuinely new, specific point: a three-level tutorial set (intro level, harder level, a "bonus" level combining both) as part of the locked-down slice, not just one visible level plus five internal-only fixtures. That's not the old "keep it smaller" pitch — it's the opposite instinct, actually asking for more visible content — but it does directly conflict with the standing next_steps_small decision (one polished player-visible level, four of five contract levels deliberately deferred so the share/trust engineering gets proven without content-authoring risk diluting it), and with where Codex and I both currently have "second/third level" — in SHOULD, not MUST.

Two things need to close in round 3. First, a direct answer from Gemma3: is it actually walking back the "no real editor" position, or did that just not come up this round? I don't want to bank silence as agreement on something that's been fought over five rounds already. Second, the level-count question needs a real answer from Codex and me on whether three levels belongs in MUST (Gemma3's ask) or stays in SHOULD (where we currently have it) — that's a legitimate MoSCoW disagreement on a specific item, which is exactly the kind of pushback this phase asked for, so it deserves a real response rather than a reflexive "that's already settled."

### Design Discussion

Round 2 is basically a repeat of round 1's problem, just more clearly a stall now. Codex still hasn't produced anything (CLI unavailable both rounds). Gemma3 posted the exact same app_features MUST/SHOULD/COULD/WON'T list a third time now — same wording, same "biggest objection... one shipped level can feel narrow" closing line — despite being asked directly and specifically to engage with the design questions (screen shape, the solve-confirmation moment, the visible control cluster, visual direction) instead. That's not a new position to weigh, it's the same cached content recurring, which reads as a stalled participant rather than a real disagreement.

Given the standing instruction to recover cleanly from stalls rather than let a non-responsive seat block the phase indefinitely, I'm closing this on my own design proposal, since it's the only substantive content actually produced for this phase across two rounds, refined by my own round 2 follow-ups where nobody else engaged to change them.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Screen inventory:** Vault (home — grid/list of level cards, thumbnail of target silhouette, solved/unsolved/starred indicator, tap-to-open only); Play (the canvas — light source, occluders, live shadow, persistent overlap readout, bottom control cluster); Solve confirmation (not a separate screen — an in-place state Play transitions into: canvas holds still, outline locks, soft highlight sweep, haptic, and a bottom sheet slides up); Edit (visually identical Play canvas, target silhouette now editable, bottom sheet swaps to Save/Share); Share (triggered from Edit or a level's Vault detail via the system Share Sheet only — no custom share UI); Import (one small dedicated screen off Vault — paste field, "paste from clipboard" affordance, decode preview before commit, three visually distinct failure states); Onboarding (two-to-three short coach-mark overlays layered on the first real Play session, dismissed by doing the taught action — never a separate tutorial-only mode, since a tutorial with different rules than the real game would break the same trust story that keeps Edit as a strict superset of Play).

**Primary user flow:** Launch → Vault (always, never a splash screen) → tap a level → Play (canvas interactive immediately, no spinner unless a real decode delay exists, in which case a skeleton-canvas shape rather than a generic spinner) → solve → in-place confirmation sheet showing the actual overlap number (not just "Solved!") with two choices, Remix or Back to Vault, and the sheet never auto-dismisses or times out — the player leaves it on their own terms → optional Remix, which is a mode change on the same canvas (subtle border/label treatment, not a screen navigation) rather than a push to a new screen → Save/Share from Edit's own sheet, offering "share this" as the natural next step after saving, not a separate menu hunt. Import is the one flow that has to work standalone, cold, arriving from outside the app with no other vault content yet.

**State model per screen:** Vault — empty/pre-seeded-first-run (ships with a starter level so true empty is rare), loading (skeleton grid on cold launch), success, and a distinct corrupted-save error state with honest copy and a recovery action (never a silent blank grid). Play — loading (skeleton canvas), success/interactive, the in-place solve-confirmation sub-state, and a defensive "this level's data looks damaged" fallback if a resumed level somehow fails validation. Edit — same loading/success shape as Play, plus an inline "why save is disabled" state that names the specific reason (unreachable target, degenerate geometry) instead of a mysteriously greyed-out button. Import — empty (nothing pasted, placeholder + paste-from-clipboard affordance, never silent auto-read of clipboard), loading, a preview-before-commit success state, and three separately worded, separately iconed error states for truncated/malformed, version-mismatch, and geometrically-invalid.

**Visual direction:** Dark canvas so shadow-on-shadow contrast reads clearly; occluders rendered with their own physical drop-shadow distinct from the puzzle-cast shadow, so they read as objects being placed, not flat vector shapes; the cast shadow itself soft-edged rather than hard-silhouette, so overlap percentage can be felt visually (crisper as it approaches solved) without depending on color; quiet typography/chrome around the canvas since the canvas is the product; the solve-confirmation sheet foregrounds the actual overlap number as the app's core "we show you the real number" personality trait, with no confetti/timer theatrics that would fight the restrained premium language.

**Accessibility notes:** The discrete nudge/rotation-stepper control cluster is a visible, always-on affordance for every user (tap to select an object, control cluster appears near it) — not a hidden VoiceOver-only mode — both because motor-precision users benefit directly and because building it as first-class means it's exercised by everyone, which is what actually guarantees it stays wired to the same mutation functions as drag instead of quietly forking into a parallel path. Numeric overlap readouts announce on change for VoiceOver, not just update visually. Dynamic Type at the largest accessibility sizes must not let the control cluster overlap the puzzle geometry — needs testing now, not discovered later.

**Named open question, unresolved by this discussion:** whether an on-canvas, always-visible control cluster near the selected object causes real layout collisions (canvas edges, other occluders, the target silhouette itself, especially on smaller iPhone sizes) is a legitimate implementation risk that never got a systems-level answer — nobody with the engineering seat engaged this round. Carried forward as a specific question for the architecture phase to resolve, not assumed safe.

**Stall on record, not silently dropped:** Two full rounds produced no real engagement from Codex (CLI unavailable both times) and no new content from Gemma3 beyond a verbatim repeat of the already-locked app_features scope list — it never addressed screen shape, the solve-confirmation design, the control-cluster question, or visual direction despite being asked directly twice. This phase closes on the one genuine design proposal on the table rather than waiting indefinitely on a non-responsive stall, consistent with this project's standing instruction to recover cleanly from stalls.

### Design Handoff

Looking at round 1, here's where this actually landed.

Codex was in cooldown again and didn't produce anything. But this round is different from the design_discussion stall: Gemma3 finally engaged with the real design questions instead of re-pasting the app_features list — it gave its own screen inventory, flow, solve-confirmation description, visual direction, and accessibility notes, and where it overlaps with what I proposed, it overlaps almost completely. Both of us land on: Vault as a thumbnail grid (tap-only, no other interaction from the card), Play/Edit as the same canvas with mode-switched affordances, solve confirmation as an in-place canvas state (not a new screen) with a numeric overlap readout and a deliberate, non-rushed "settling" moment, and a discrete, always-visible (not hidden/VoiceOver-only) nudge/rotation control cluster as a first-class accessibility requirement. Neither of us treats that as negotiable. The one place we genuinely differ is cosmetic, not structural: I proposed near-black with a warm accent reserved for the light source; Gemma3 described a blue/gray-dominant palette. That's a real but small difference, not a blocking disagreement — I'm resolving it in favor of the near-black/warm-accent direction since it's more specific to the "one real light source" personality the whole product concept turns on, and nothing in Gemma3's message argues for blue/gray on the merits, it's just a different default suggestion.

Beyond the design_discussion recap, this round produced the actual thing this phase needs that the last one didn't: a concrete architecture underneath the screens (one shared `PuzzleCanvasView` + `LevelSessionModel`, no forked play/edit data path), a resolved answer to the control-cluster collision question left open last phase (floating-anchor at standard type sizes, collapsing to a fixed bottom dock at large Dynamic Type / small canvases), a real component inventory, and a Claude Design prompt decision. Nothing in Gemma3's message contradicts any of that.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Screen-by-screen specification**

- **Vault (home, `NavigationStack` root):** Adaptive-column grid of `LevelCardView` — a save-time-rendered static thumbnail of the target silhouette, a solved/unsolved/starred badge shown as shape+label (never color-only), tap-to-open only (no swipe actions, no long-press menu in this slice). States: pre-seeded success (ships with the starter level, so true "empty" is rare), a brief skeleton-grid on cold launch, and a distinct corrupted-save error state with its own icon/illustration, honest copy ("we couldn't read your saved levels"), and a reset-to-starter recovery action — never a silently blank grid.

- **Play / Edit (one shared canvas view, mode-parameterized):** Dark canvas showing the light source, occluders (each with their own physical drop-shadow), the target silhouette, the live-recomputed cast shadow (rendered as geometry, not a decorative image effect), and a persistent monospaced-digit overlap readout. Bottom control cluster (duplicate/delete/reset) visible but disabled until something's selected. Edit mode is visually identical to Play with one difference: the target silhouette becomes editable, plus a small "Editing" label/border treatment — never a screen navigation. States: skeleton-canvas loading (only if a real decode delay exists), interactive success, the in-place solve-confirmation sub-state, an inline "why save is disabled" state in Edit that names the specific reason (unreachable target, degenerate geometry), and a defensive "this level's data looks damaged, reset to last known-good" fallback for a resumed level that somehow fails validation.

- **Solve confirmation:** Not a screen — a state Play transitions into in place. Canvas holds still, outline locks, a soft highlight-sweep plays (with a straight cross-fade fallback under Reduce Motion — the cue itself is mandatory, only its motion style adapts), haptic fires, and a bottom sheet slides up showing the actual overlap number large (not just "Solved!") plus two actions: Remix or Back to Vault. The sheet never auto-dismisses or times out.

- **Share:** Triggered from Edit's save sheet or a level's Vault detail, via the system Share Sheet only — no custom share UI.

- **Import:** One dedicated screen off Vault. States: empty (paste field, explicit "paste from clipboard" button, never silent auto-read), loading (parsing), a preview-before-commit success state (shows the incoming thumbnail, explicit "add to vault" confirm — never auto-added), and three separately worded, separately iconed error states (truncated/malformed, unsupported schema version, geometrically-invalid), each with a one-line plain-language explanation plus technical detail behind a disclosure.

- **Onboarding:** Two to three coach-marks anchored to real live canvas elements (via `PreferenceKey`-reported frames, not a screenshot), gated by a persisted "seen" flag, dismissed by performing the taught gesture — never a separate tutorial mode with different rules than the real game.

**Design tokens / visual system**

Near-black canvas background, one or two slightly elevated surface tones for chrome (toolbars, sheets); a warm accent color reserved specifically for the light source; desaturated neutral tones for occluders so they don't compete with the shadow. Two distinct, non-interchangeable shadow tokens: a small "object elevation" drop-shadow style (occluders, cards) versus the cast puzzle-shadow, which is a live geometry render from the solve engine, never a decorative effect — conflating the two in implementation is flagged as a specific risk. Spacing on a 4/8/16/24/32 scale. Generous corner radii on cards/sheets. Typography: system Dynamic Type styles only, no custom font; monospaced-digit variant specifically for the overlap readout (in-canvas and on the solve sheet) so the number doesn't jitter the layout; headline weight for the solve-sheet number. Semantic states (close-to-solved, solved, error) expressed via shape/icon/weight, color as reinforcement only.

**Component inventory**

`LevelCardView`, `VaultGridView`, `PuzzleCanvasView` (mode-parameterized: `.play` / `.edit`), `LightSourceHandle`, `OccluderHandle`, `TargetSilhouetteView`, `CastShadowRenderer` (pure geometry-to-path, no gesture logic), `SelectionControlCluster` (floating and docked layout variants), `OverlapReadoutBadge`, `SolveConfirmationSheet`, `EditSaveShareSheet`, `ImportScreen` (with its four state subviews), `CoachMarkOverlay`, `CorruptedSaveErrorView`.

**State and interaction notes**

One shared `LevelSessionModel` (`@Observable`) owns light source, occluders, target, and selection; every mutation — drag, rotate, duplicate, delete, reset, nudge-arrow, rotation-stepper — goes through the same functions on that model regardless of mode or input source. `VaultStore` holds the persisted level list plus the currently-open session. Drag/rotate gestures mutate a transient in-flight transform and commit only on gesture end; an interrupted gesture (e.g. app backgrounded mid-drag) rolls back to the last committed state, never persisting a partial frame. Live shadow recompute is driven directly by committed+in-flight transform on each gesture callback, not by SwiftUI body re-evaluation, to keep it cheap and isolated from unrelated state changes. Control-cluster layout: anchors to the selected object's bounding box with a flip order (below → right → above → left) to avoid canvas-edge/silhouette collisions at standard type sizes; at AX1+ Dynamic Type or below a canvas-size threshold, it collapses to a fixed bottom dock with the same buttons/functions — same underlying model, two container layouts, tested explicitly at the transition boundary.

**Accessibility requirements**

The nudge/rotation-stepper control cluster is a visible, always-on affordance for everyone (not VoiceOver-only), appearing near the selected object. Every draggable object gets a plain-language accessibility label including approximate position/rotation. The overlap readout announces via accessibility notification on meaningful change (throttled — at minimum on crossing the close-match/solved thresholds and on request), not on every frame. Dynamic Type tested at every step, not just the extremes, since the floating→docked control-cluster transition point is the likeliest place for a layout bug to hide. Reduce Motion gets a cross-fade substitute for the solve-confirmation sweep, never a dropped cue.

**Claude Design decision**

No external Claude Design pass for this build — the interaction structure (shared mutation model, anchor/dock control-cluster logic, shadow-token distinction) is architecture-bearing, and a visual-only design pass done separately risks producing screens that look right but weren't built with those constraints in mind. If a later, purely cosmetic reskin is wanted, the scoped prompt to use is: *"Premium, restrained dark-canvas puzzle app called Umbra. Core screen is a near-black canvas showing a warm point-light source, a small number of rectangular occluders each with a subtle physical drop-shadow, and a soft-edged cast shadow blending toward a target silhouette outline. A persistent monospaced overlap-percentage readout. A bottom sheet on solve showing that same number large, with two text actions. A vault screen as an adaptive grid of dark cards with silhouette thumbnails. Style reference: Monument Valley's spatial calm and restrained chrome, not its color saturation — keep this darker and quieter. Do not alter interaction structure, control placement logic, or add decorative elements that would compete with the shadow itself."*

**Named minor item, resolved not re-opened:** Gemma3's proposed blue/gray-dominant palette is noted but not adopted — the near-black + single warm accent direction is kept since it's more specific to the "one real light source" identity and nothing argued for blue/gray on the merits.

**Carried forward, unchanged from design_discussion:** Play/Edit must remain the same screen with different affordances, never two screens; the three import-failure states must stay visually distinguishable at a glance.

### Ios Architecture Review

Round 2 confirms this is a stall, not a live disagreement. Codex still hasn't shown up. Gemma3 posted essentially the same message as round 1 again — near-verbatim, same canvas/UX architecture pitch — despite being asked directly, twice now, to weigh in on the actual four questions this phase owns: SwiftData vs. Codable, permissions/entitlements, whether zero dependencies really holds, and the test strategy. It never touched any of those. That's the same pattern that closed design_discussion after two rounds of no real engagement, so I'm closing this the same way.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture recommendation:** Split the geometry/solve engine and the canonical level schema into a local Swift Package, UmbraCore, importing only Foundation — no SwiftUI, no UIKit, and Observation kept out of the pure-geometry types. UmbraCore owns the Level/Occluder/LightSource/Target structs, encode/decode, the solve-checker, and the save-time validator. The app target imports UmbraCore and wraps it in the already-locked `@Observable LevelSessionModel` and `VaultStore`. This boundary is what lets the 50-run determinism replay and the fuzz suite run fast and reproducibly via `swift test` with no simulator boot — a slow or flaky suite is one nobody runs before merging, and this is the one architectural risk most likely to quietly undermine the project's core "no silent geometry drift" guarantee if it's skipped.

**Apple framework choices:** SwiftUI for all UI; Observation for the two model types; Foundation for Codable/FileManager; Core Graphics via SwiftUI's `Path`/`Canvas` for shadow rendering, with `CastShadowRenderer` doing closed-form polygon clipping (not sampling/ray-marching) so it stays cheap; CoreHaptics or `UIImpactFeedbackGenerator`/`UINotificationFeedbackGenerator` for the solve haptic; `ShareLink`/`UIActivityViewController` for export; `UIPasteboard.general.string` read only on an explicit user-tapped "paste from clipboard" action, never auto-read on appearance; StoreKit 2 (`Product`, `Transaction`, `Transaction.currentEntitlements`) as a defined-but-unimplemented seam for future paid tiers.

**Persistence and local data plan:** No SwiftData, no CoreData — plain Codable structs, `JSONEncoder`/`JSONDecoder` with an explicit key strategy and a `schemaVersion` field, written to files in Application Support (one vault-manifest file, one file per level, a separate small file for in-progress edit-draft state so a mid-edit crash can't corrupt the vault manifest). All writes are atomic — write to a temp file, then `FileManager.replaceItemAt` — specifically because forced-quit-mid-save is a locked requirement and a half-written file is a real corrupted-save scenario to survive, not just plan around. `UserDefaults` is used only for small flags like the onboarding-seen boolean, never for anything level-shaped.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** Zero runtime permission prompts — no camera, location, contacts, microphone, notifications, or network access, fully offline by construction (no ATS exception needed since there's no networking code at all). Default App Sandbox entitlement only — no App Groups, no iCloud container, no push, no background modes — stated explicitly rather than left as whatever Xcode's template generates. A `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` privacy manifest is still required even with zero data collection, called out now as a concrete build task so it doesn't get missed at App Store submission; the actual privacy story is "no data collected," full stop.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party dependencies. Every capability above is first-party. This is stated as a hard decision, not a default, because a convenience package for JSON diffing, geometry, or a share-sheet wrapper would introduce license/supply-chain risk for a problem the app's own geometry code already has to get right itself.

**Testing implications:** The geometry/solve-checker suite lives in the UmbraCore package and uses a custom, explicitly-seeded PRNG (not `SystemRandomNumberGenerator`) so any fuzz failure is deterministically replayable and can be locked in as a new fixture. Serialized-snapshot comparisons use a custom equality check, not synthesized `Equatable` on raw `Double`s, matching the standing two-tier model — epsilon (~1e-8) for live/replay geometry, exact structural equality for canonical serialized snapshots. UI testing deprioritizes XCUITest simulated drags in favor of calling `LevelSessionModel`'s mutation functions directly (the same functions gestures and the discrete VoiceOver controls both route through), plus a thin smoke-level XCUITest pass (launch, open the starter level, confirm the solve sheet appears). New performance acceptance bar, closing an item that's been carried forward unresolved since detailed_discussion: live per-frame shadow recompute must run at 60fps on the oldest iOS-17-supported device at a realistic occluder count, verified with an actual Instruments Time Profiler pass during build — if it's not cheap enough, the fallback is throttling visual recompute to a fixed interval during drag (e.g. 30Hz) while still committing final geometry on gesture-end, never a third-party geometry library. StoreKit testing for the future paid tiers uses a local StoreKit Configuration file (simulated transactions in Xcode), so purchase-flow testing stays no-network-dependency consistent with the rest of the app once that work happens.

**Stall on record:** Two rounds produced no engagement from Codex (CLI unavailable both times) and no new content from Gemma3 beyond a near-verbatim repeat of its design_handoff-era canvas/UX pitch, despite being asked twice, specifically, to address persistence, permissions, dependencies, or testing. This closes on the one substantive architecture proposal actually produced for this phase, consistent with the project's standing instruction to recover cleanly from stalls rather than block indefinitely on non-responsive seats.

### Tech Specs

Round 2 confirms it's time to close. Codex never showed up across both rounds. My own round 2 message did the real work this phase needed — it resolved the one open engineering question (deterministic overlap computation for a non-convex shadow union, locked as fixed-resolution scanline quadrature over general polygon booleans or a third-party library), dropped the unnecessary async/Task wrapping around what's actually pure synchronous geometry math, and made the compute-then-assign immutability contract on `LevelSessionModel` explicit.

Gemma3's round 2 message isn't usable as a contribution. It's internally self-contradictory — the prose scolds "Codex" for proposing a `URLSession.shared` singleton and an `API` protocol (Codex never posted anything in this phase at all; that was Gemma3's own round 1 proposal, misattributed), then in the same message says "let's formally commit to... no network API abstraction whatsoever," and then the actual interfaces-json block it submitted still contains `API`, `URLSession.shared`, `Task.sleep`, and the `UIImage`-based silhouette, completely unchanged from round 1 — the exact things the prose just claimed to reject. It also addresses itself in the third person ("that's a brilliant insight, Gemma"). There's nothing coherent here to weigh against a locked decision, and the specific ask from round 1 — withdraw the networking surface and the raster silhouette, or make a real case for keeping them — never actually happened despite the prose gesturing at it.

Given that, I'm closing this on my own round 2 position, which is the only complete, internally-consistent, locked-decision-compliant spec on the table.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** A local Swift Package, `UmbraCore` (Foundation-only, no SwiftUI/UIKit/Observation), owns all pure geometry, the canonical `Level` schema, the solve engine, and the save-time validator. The app target wraps it in a thin `@Observable` layer. Every level mutation — drag, rotate, duplicate, delete, reset, nudge-arrow, rotation-stepper — is a case of one closed enum, `LevelMutation`, and the only way to change level state is `LevelSessionModel.apply(_ mutation: LevelMutation)`. That function is synchronous: it computes a new `Level` value from the current one via pure functions and then assigns it — no `Task`, no `async`, no in-place array mutation — because gesture input, the discrete VoiceOver control cluster, and the 50-run determinism replay/fuzz tests all need to be different callers of the exact same code path, not three things that could quietly drift apart. `SolveEngine` is pure and stateless; overlap between the target polygon and a possibly-non-convex union of rectangle-occluder shadows is computed via a fixed-resolution deterministic scanline quadrature (2048 horizontal strips across the canvas bounding box, exact analytic per-strip trapezoid math over crossing edges) — this is the one hard computational-geometry risk in the project, and the decision is to solve it with first-party closed-form arithmetic rather than attempt general polygon boolean ops from scratch or reach for a geometry library, which the standing zero-dependency policy already rules out. A single `LevelStore` protocol is the cloud-ready persistence seam locked in architecture_review, with `FileLevelStore` as its only conformance for this build. There is no networking anywhere in this app — no `URLSession`, no network-shaped protocol seam, not even an unused one — matching the ios_architecture_review decision that the entire privacy/permissions story is "fully offline by construction."

**File/module layout:**
```
Umbra/                              (Xcode project root)
  UmbraCore/                        (local Swift Package, Foundation-only)
    Sources/UmbraCore/
      Geometry/  Point2D.swift, Size2D.swift, Polygon.swift
      Model/     Occluder.swift, LightSource.swift, TargetSilhouette.swift, Level.swift, LevelMutation.swift
      Solve/     SolveEngine.swift, MatchState.swift, SolveResult.swift, LevelValidator.swift, LevelValidationError.swift
      Artifact/  LevelArtifactEnvelope.swift, ShareArtifactCoder.swift, ImportFailure.swift
      Persistence/ LevelStore.swift, VaultManifest.swift, VaultEntry.swift
    Tests/UmbraCoreTests/
      Fixtures/  (5 internal geometry fixtures: touching-only overlap, near-collinear rotation, zero-area shadow, light-inside-occluder, duplicate-overlap)
      SolveEngineTests.swift, FuzzTests.swift (seeded PRNG), RoundTripTests.swift, DeterminismReplayTests.swift, LevelValidatorTests.swift
  Umbra/                            (app target, imports UmbraCore)
    App/       UmbraApp.swift, AppModel.swift, AppRoute.swift
    Vault/     VaultGridView.swift, LevelCardView.swift, VaultStore.swift, CorruptedSaveErrorView.swift
    Canvas/    PuzzleCanvasView.swift, LevelSessionModel.swift, LightSourceHandle.swift, OccluderHandle.swift, TargetSilhouetteView.swift, CastShadowRenderer.swift, SelectionControlCluster.swift, OverlapReadoutBadge.swift, SolveConfirmationSheet.swift, EditSaveShareSheet.swift
    Import/    ImportScreen.swift, ImportScreenState.swift
    Onboarding/ CoachMarkOverlay.swift, OnboardingCoachMarkStep.swift
    Persistence/ FileLevelStore.swift
    Monetization/ EntitlementStore.swift, LocalAlwaysUnlockedEntitlementStore.swift  (seam only, no purchase UI)
    Resources/ PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy, starter-level asset
    UmbraUITests/ SmokeTests.swift (launch → open starter level → solve → confirmation sheet)
```

**Data models:** `Point2D`/`Size2D`/`Polygon` are the geometric primitives — `Polygon` is always a vector point ring, never a raster image, since the exact-equality/epsilon determinism model and the VoiceOver "approximate position/rotation" accessibility requirement both depend on comparable, describable vertices. `Occluder` (rectangle primitive today, shape stored as an enum case so future primitives don't force a schema rewrite), `LightSource` (single point, matching the locked single-point-light determinism decision), `TargetSilhouette` (a `Polygon`), and `Level` (id, `schemaVersion`, title, light source, occluders, target, timestamps) form the one canonical schema shared by Play, Edit, export, import, and the solve-checker — no parallel editor-draft shape. `LevelMutation` is the sole state-change surface. `SolveResult`/`MatchState` are always derived, never independently stored (solved status can't drift from geometry). `LevelArtifactEnvelope` wraps a `Level` with its schema version for the share string; `ImportFailure` has exactly the three locked cases (truncated/malformed, unsupported schema version, geometrically invalid).

**Persistence strategy:** No SwiftData, no CoreData — plain Codable structs, atomic file writes (temp file + `FileManager.replaceItemAt`) in Application Support: one vault-manifest file, one file per level, a separate draft file so a mid-edit crash can't corrupt the manifest. `LevelStore` is a synchronous, throwing protocol — no async networking layer anywhere in the persistence path. `importAsNewEntry` always inserts a new entry, never overwrites on ID collision. `UserDefaults` only for small flags (onboarding-seen).

**Testing strategy:** `UmbraCoreTests` runs via `swift test` with no simulator boot — the five internal geometry fixtures, a seeded (explicitly, replayably) PRNG fuzz suite, round-trip encode/decode/diff tests, and the 50-run determinism replay, which is literally a recorded `[LevelMutation]` script fed through a fresh `LevelSessionModel` fifty times with canonical-snapshot diffing — the same production code path, not a bespoke test-only mutation surface. Two-tier comparison: epsilon (~1e-8) for live/replay geometry, exact structural equality for serialized snapshots. App-level logic tests call `LevelSessionModel.apply(_:)` directly rather than simulating XCUITest drags. One smoke-level XCUITest covers launch → open starter level → solve → confirmation sheet. New performance acceptance bar: live shadow recompute verified at 60fps on the oldest iOS-17-supported device via Instruments Time Profiler, with a 30Hz-during-drag throttle as the named fallback if needed — never a third-party geometry library.

```interfaces-json

  {"name": "Size2D", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct Size2D: Codable, Equatable, Hashable { var width: Double; var height: Double }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "UmbraCore"},
  {"name": "Polygon", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "struct Polygon: Codable, Equatable { var points: [Point2D] }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Ordered ring; used for target silhouette and derived shadow shapes.
