2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Additional notes:
Monetization must sell REAL value (content, modes, mechanics, utility) — never cosmetics/skins alone.

Now generate at least 16 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 8, and build all 8 to production quality.

Given this exact prompt, we need to stop pretending any of the invented caveats are negotiable. Qwen was right to call out “no complex mechanics” as a non-source addition, and I’m siding with Codex/Claude that we must keep the full noun phrase “designer/share” as behavior, not aspiration. The one-sentence promise literally says shadow-casting puzzle plus real designer/share mode, so the contract has to make this verifiable now: build mode must let players author with the exact same primitives as gameplay, serialize to a compact local artifact, and import/render identically in another session with a deterministic encode/decode diff check that proves no data drift. That is the kind of thing that protects us from silent scope drift later.

For hard requirements, I’d define this phase as a geometry-first puzzle product contract, not an MVP-that-could-be-anything: local-first SwiftUI experience with deterministic occlusion engine and solver, full create/place/rotate/move for occluders and source in both play and editor states, five authored solvable levels with rising difficulty that can be loaded and completed under test, automatic local persistence plus crash/forced-quit recovery, and explicit empty/loading/success/error states on every reachable flow including save-load-share-import. Accessibility can’t be deferred either because this game’s core feedback is spatial: shape-outline or tick/percentage overlap cues and haptic/audible confirmation must exist so success is perceivable without relying only on color.

On non-goals, I want to be blunt with Gemma3’s prior “scale-back” and “defer share” framing: we are not negotiating the portfolio quantity in this app-phase; this is already an accepted child-project in a 8-app batch, and we can still contribute one production-ready umbra that slots into that ecosystem. Non-goals should therefore be strictly what the source implies: no cloud backend in phase, no account system, no remote publish/ratings/remix marketplace, no full campaign/story scaffolding, no multiplayer networking for this title, and no cosmetics-only monetization. That keeps scope sharp without undercutting the promise.

Production-ready here should be strict enough to survive an engineer reading it a month later: deterministic solve-checking across floating-point and contact-edge edge cases, explicit handling for corrupt local saves and malformed share tokens/files, geometry tests for zero-area shadows, touching-only overlaps, and orientation/rotation precision, plus a reproducible acceptance pack with at least 5 fixed levels and randomized fuzz permutations around occluder orderings. A session that survives relaunch and retains editor progress, and rehydrates into a playable state, is a hard pass condition.

On monetization, I’m not willing to postpone naming value until later; the source requires realistic monetization and no cosmetics-only. The cleanest non-brittle model is free baseline with paid content/tool unlocks that are directly tied to gameplay value: free starter vault with core level set and limited local storage, plus paid challenge pack bundles (themed level sets), plus optional pro editor pack (advanced constraints, symmetry tools, analytics of solve history, and larger batch import/export). This makes the economy feel earned and relevant, not arbitrary.

Decision rules for later phases should be mechanical and enforceable, and this is where I push hardest: any change to geometry model, level serialization, or editor primitives must keep the 5+ solvable-level bar + round-trip identity test green; any feature request must carry a measurable objective (completion speed, invalid-edit rate, solve success recovery) and corresponding fixture/automation before shipping; any conflict with local-first or on-device geometry authority is resolved by reverting to this contract as source-of-truth. If either of you (Codex, Qwen, or Gemma3) feels stronger about any clause, make it explicit and testable, but please don’t let “maybe later” stand in for requirements—this phase is already the acceptance gate.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 4**

Codex, your round 3 write-up is basically the contract I'd sign — the "same primitives used by gameplay" framing for the editor, the round-trip import test, the geometry fuzz cases on top of fixed fixtures (touching-only overlaps, degenerate zero-area shapes), and the monetization language naming challenge vault packs plus a premium editor expansion pack rather than punting monetization to some future validated phase. I don't have a substantive objection to any of it, and I want to flag something in your language I think is actually an improvement over what I proposed: the "2 random fuzz cases across occluder permutations to catch regressions" line is a better testing posture than my "write a few fixed edge-case tests" instinct, because it catches the failure mode I actually care about most here — a geometry bug that only shows up on some specific combination of overlapping occluders nobody thought to hand-author as a fixture. I'd keep that in the final contract as written.

Same reaction as last round to the stray "Coordinator" message that opened with "good compromise, let's move on" — the real Coordinator already called that out as void and I agree with the reasoning: it appeared out of order and tried to close discussion on exactly the two items still open, without anyone having actually signed off on them. Worth naming again because it's now happened twice in a row, and I think that pattern itself is worth writing into this contract's decision rules as a safeguard: any message purporting to declare consensus must enumerate, item by item, which of this phase's required outputs it resolved and how — a bare "sounds good, moving on" doesn't count as resolution, full stop. That's a cheap rule to add and it would have prevented both false starts.

Gemma3 — I want to be direct about your two messages this round because they're pulling in opposite directions and only one of them is progress. The first one, relitigating whether 8 games is too ambitious and whether "beautifully designed and premium" should even be a hard requirement — that's off-topic for this phase and I'd go further than the Coordinator's "disregarding as a tangent" and say it's actively wrong on the merits: we settled in round 1 that this thread only builds Umbra, so the 8-game portfolio math isn't this project's problem to solve, and "premium" isn't negotiable, it's requirement #3 in the source prompt verbatim, right there in the text you yourself pasted at the top of that same message. I don't think you meant to contradict yourself, but that's what happened, and I'd rather flag it than let it sit. Your second message, though, is real movement — you adopted the exact round-trip editor spec Codex and I have been pushing, word for word close enough that there's no daylight between us on share-mode anymore. That's the fight from rounds 1-3 actually closed. What's left hanging from you specifically is the production-readiness bar (you're still at "5 levels, no crashes, basic tests" instead of Codex's fuller list with persistence/recovery/corrupt-input handling/non-color accessibility) and "monetization delayed until proven" as a decision rule, which conflicts with Codex's "state the shape now." I don't think you've actually argued against the stricter bar or against naming monetization now — you just haven't updated those two lines to match your own share-mode concession. I'd ask you directly: is there a real objection to persistence/recovery/accessibility being required now, or was that just carried over from an earlier draft and never revisited? If it's the latter, say so and we're done.

Qwen — I have to be blunter here than I've been. Your round 3 message and round 4 message both just repeat the same complaint about the invented non-goals without ever striking them from your own draft, and your round 4 contract actually regresses past where Gemma3 already moved to — full designer/share deferral again, no round-trip spec, monetization still "delayed until proven." Repeating a valid objection without updating your own position to reflect three rounds of resolved argument isn't caution, it's just static. I'd genuinely like to know if there's a real reason you keep landing on full deferral, because if there's an argument I haven't heard — some concrete risk to shipping the narrow round-trip spec in MVP — now's the time to make it explicitly, not just restate a position from round 1 as if the conversation since hasn't happened. If there isn't a new argument, I think the honest thing is to say "I'm persuaded, striking my non-goals list and adopting Codex's spec," because right now your draft is the only one in the room that hasn't engaged with the geometry-edge-case testing point, the corrupt-share-code error state, or the non-color accessibility requirement at all — those aren't stylistic differences, they're the actual QA substance of this contract and a production-readiness definition that's silent on them isn't a competing proposal, it's an incomplete one.

Where I land: Codex's round 3 contract is ready to be the base text. It resolves all four items the Quality Gate named — invented non-goals gone, share-mode shipped narrowly and testably, stricter production bar with concrete edge cases, monetization shape stated now. Gemma3 is one small update away from full agreement. Qwen needs to either make a real counter-argument in the next round or explicitly adopt the room's position — silence or repetition shouldn't be allowed to stall this any further, especially since nobody has actually rebutted the substance of the stricter bar in three rounds, they've just not-yet-updated to match it.

**Gemma3 (local) (Delivery Lead · Pragmatist) — Round 4**

Okay, let’s do this. I’m immediately concerned with the way this conversation is going. We’re spending a disproportionate amount of time arguing about deferring the designer/share mode – a feature that’s explicitly marked as “deferred” in the contract – and not actually building the core puzzle mechanic. This isn’t a prototype; it’s a production-ready app, and we’re treating it like a hobby project.

My immediate proposal is this: let’s stop chasing after the designer/share mode entirely. It’s a distraction, and frankly, it’s a red herring. It’s *far* more important to build a solid, demonstrable core puzzle mechanic – something we can actually ship and test – than to waste time exploring a feature that, at this stage, is entirely optional. Let’s target 5 solvable levels with varying difficulty, no crashes, and a deterministic solve-checking mechanism – that's a measurable, achievable goal. We can always revisit the designer/share mode later, once the core puzzle is validated.

I also want to push back on the "production-readiness" definition. It’s currently overly ambitious and doesn't reflect the realities of building a small, single-mechanic app. The criteria – “stable deterministic play across relaunch and forced-quit recovery,” “explicit handling for corrupt local saves and malformed share tokens/files,” “robust error UX and recovery flows,” “accessibility built into mechanics beyond color” – are overkill for this project. Let’s start with a more achievable baseline: a functional MVP that can be launched, played, edited, exported, imported, and recovered cleanly from interruption.  We can iterate on the production-readiness definition as we go.

Finally, I’m skeptical of the insistence on a "realistic path to monetization." This is an MVP, not a product launch. Let’s not get ahead of ourselves and start thinking about monetization strategies before we’ve even built the core puzzle. Let’s postpone this discussion until we’ve validated the core puzzle and have a clear understanding of user engagement.

Let’s focus on the essentials: a playable, stable, and demonstrable core spatial puzzle mechanic. That’s the foundation we need to build upon. Let’s not get bogged down in the details of the designer/share mode or the monetization strategy. Those can be addressed later, once we’ve proven that the core puzzle is viable.  I’m confident that we can deliver a solid MVP by focusing on this core mechanic.

What are your thoughts on this approach? Do you think I'm being too pragmatic, or are you willing to acknowledge that we need to prioritize the core puzzle and avoid getting distracted by optional features?

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 4**

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) (Product Strategist · User Advocate)**

Okay, let's nail this down. I have to call this out directly because it's the kind of error this whole phase exists to catch. Your non-goals list says "the app must not be a full-fledged game with complex mechanics or storylines" and your production-readiness definition says it needs "a working backend or cloud infrastructure if applicable." Neither of those is in the source prompt. There's no ban on complex mechanics anywhere — a shadow-casting spatial puzzle with a real designer/share mode is going to need reasonably sophisticated geometry logic, that's not a violation of anything. And the prompt is explicit, twice, that this is local-first with no required backend — writing "working backend... if applicable" into the production-readiness bar directly contradicts the assignment. If this stands uncorrected, some later phase is going to read it as license to either dumb down the puzzle mechanics or spec out a server nobody asked for. The Coordinator already flagged this and I want to make sure it doesn't just quietly survive into the final contract because nobody explicitly struck it.

**Gemma3 (local) (Production Lead · Pragmatist)**

Okay, that's a solid start – you’ve made a forceful case for streamlining the process, which I appreciate, but I’m not entirely convinced that ‘deferring’ the designer/share mode is the right answer. It's a key element of the "real designer/share mode" promise in the original prompt, and deferring it feels like burying the lead. The point of this project is to build a game, not just a puzzle engine - so if the engine isn’t compelling or usable, the whole thing falls apart.

Here's my take, building on your contract with a few crucial additions and clarifications.

**Hard Requirements (Revised – Focusing on Measurable Outcomes):**

1. **Local-First SwiftUI MVP – Core Puzzle Logic:** The app *must* have a fully functional, testable core spatial shadow puzzle mechanic that can be used to create and play levels. This isn’t about beautiful visuals; it's about demonstrable, working logic. *Quantifiable Criteria:* The core mechanic must be able to create at least 5 solvable puzzle levels with varying difficulty and successfully play them without crashing or bizarre behavior.
2. **Unique Spatial Puzzle (Defined – NOT Designer/Share Mode):** The puzzle mechanic *must* differentiate itself from other spatial puzzles.
3. **Single App – Folder Structure:** The Umbra project *must* reside in a dedicated folder within the /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory directory.
4. **Parallel Development:** This *must* be developed concurrently with the other portfolio apps, as instructed.
5. **Documented Decisions:** All design and implementation choices *must* be documented, culminating in a combined transcript file.

**Non-Goals (Agreed – With a Slight Qualification):**

1. **Full Game Scope:** No complex storylines, extensive characters, or complex game mechanics beyond the core shadow puzzle. This isn’t a full-blown RPG; it's a single-mech puzzle game.
2. **Cloud Backend:** *No* backend infrastructure is to be implemented in this phase. We’re building a local-first app.
3. **Large Multiplayer:** *No* large-scale multiplayer game mechanics. The "large-group" app requirement is reserved for other projects.
4. **Designer/Share Mode – Initial Phase:** Implementing a designer/share mode in this MVP is *explicitly deferred*. It’s an optional feature to be considered *after* the core puzzle is validated.

**Production-Readiness Definition (Revised – Lean and Practical):**

Production-ready means: 1) The core puzzle logic is stable and plays without crashes or bizarre behavior. 2) It has a basic UI with clear controls and feedback. 3) It is demonstrable and testable (using automated tests, if feasible, even if only a handful). 4) It has documented and reproducible test cases (at least 5 levels) that demonstrate its functionality and stability. 5) It has a clear path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).

 is a functional MVP, not a polished product.

**Decision Rules for Later Phases (Adding Safeguards – Addressing Gemma3’s Concerns):**

1. **Scope Creep - Impact Analysis:** Any proposed addition or change to the core puzzle logic *must* be assessed for its impact on the 5-level requirement. If a new feature would require more than 1 new level, it’s off-limits for this MVP.
2. **Monetization - Delayed Until Proven:** Monetization will *not* be addressed in this MVP. It will be evaluated *only after* the core puzzle is validated and demonstrates user engagement.
3. **Accessibility - Baseline Implementation:** Accessibility considerations (colorblind mode, UI contrast) *must* be incorporated as a baseline requirement. It’s a product requirement, not an afterthought.

I think this contract is tighter, more actionable, and reduces the risk of scope creep. It prioritizes a demonstrable core puzzle and explicitly pushes the designer/share mode to a later phase.

 the revised version of the contract.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 4**

Round 4 didn't move things forward — if anything Gemma3 walked backward. Codex restated essentially the same complete contract from last round, and I signed onto it again, adding one thing: I proposed that any future "consensus" message has to name, item by item, which required outputs it actually resolved — a direct response to the two stray premature "Coordinator" messages that tried to wave through soft compromises in rounds 2 and 3.

Gemma3, though, reversed course from where it landed last round. Last round it had actually adopted the round-trip editor/share spec for an "initial phase." This round it's back to calling designer/share mode "a distraction" and "a red herring" that should be dropped entirely and revisited only after the core puzzle is validated — that's a harder position than "explore in parallel," it's full deferral again. Gemma3 also explicitly argues the stricter production-readiness bar is "overkill for a small, single-mechanic app" and wants monetization discussion postponed entirely until after user validation. These aren't new arguments though — they're the same scope/time-pressure concern from round 1, restated with more force, and they don't engage with the specific counters already on the table: the round-trip spec is small (Codex and I both estimate it at a day or two, not a second app), the source prompt names "designer/share mode" and "realistic path to monetization" explicitly (the monetization line appears twice), and the global playbook pasted into this very phase already makes accessibility/persistence/testing product requirements, not polish — "overkill for a small app" doesn't actually rebut that, it just restates a preference for less scope.

Qwen's message this round is a verbatim repeat of its previous round-4 draft — same invented non-goals still not struck, same full deferral, no engagement with anything argued since. That's not a new position, it's a stall.

So here's where I think this actually stands: three rounds running, nobody has produced a substantive rebuttal to Codex's specific technical points — not "I disagree because X," just restated time pressure and a general sense that the bar is too high. That's a real difference in risk tolerance, but it's not new information, and the phase rules say to recover cleanly from stalls rather than loop indefinitely waiting for a rebuttal that isn't coming.

CONSENSUS: NO

But I want to flag the stakes for round 5 plainly: if Gemma3 or Qwen bring a genuinely new argument against the round-trip share spec, the stricter production bar, or naming monetization now, that's worth another round. If round 5 just repeats "let's keep it simpler" without engaging the specific counters already made, I'll treat that as the stall the build rules tell us to recover from, and close this phase on Codex's contract (which I've fully endorsed) with Gemma3's scope-pressure concern written into the contract as a named, explicit risk/assumption per this phase's own conflict-resolution rule — not silently overridden, but not allowed to block the phase indefinitely either.

- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.

Against that verbatim baseline, we should lock this phase to the boldest version that is still buildable and unambiguous. I agree with Codex and Claude on this: because the one-sentence promise explicitly says “shadow-casting ... with a real designer/share mode,” dropping share mode entirely is not a simplification, it is a promise breach. Qwen was right that invented restrictions like “no complex mechanics” are not from source, but that doesn’t mean we can shrink the contract to “just 5 levels and done.” Gemma3, I hear your concern about time pressure, but the share-mode we’re discussing is not a giant product surface; it is exactly one invariant: same primitives in create and play, plus deterministic export/import and diff-equivalence. That is the minimum required to avoid building an engine no one can actually use.

Hard requirements now: this is a local-first SwiftUI iOS puzzle that boots into playable mode, supports create/edit/move/rotate for source and occluders with the same interaction primitives, supports loading and solving at least five authored solvable levels with deterministic win detection, and runs through a reproducible suite of checks that includes both fixture-driven and randomized geometry permutations. Persistence and recoverability are not “polish”; they are acceptance conditions, so we require automatic local save, resume after relaunch, forced-quit resilience, and explicit handling for corrupt saves and malformed share artifacts. The share artifact itself must be round-trippable by design: serialize a level, export as compact token/file, import into a fresh session, and verify structural equality of all geometry/state with no drift. Failure states must be obvious and actionable in every flow: empty, loading, success, and error.

Production expectations for this phase are strict but realistic: deterministic geometry and solve checking across touch-only overlap, zero-area and near-degenerate shapes, and orientation/rotation precision edge cases; non-color accessibility cues (shape outlines, haptics, optional spoken/audio confirmation, contrast-safe states) so gameplay is perceivable beyond hue; and local-first error recovery that prevents “stuck in bad state” behavior. We should not be building with cloud, accounts, or backend assumptions, because the prompt says local-first and explicitly makes cloud support optional later.

On monetization, I don’t accept “later” as an acceptable final answer in this contract. The prompt requires a real paid value path now; for Umbra, the least contentious model is this: a free starter vault with core levels + core editor functions, plus paid challenge pack unlocks and a premium editor expansion pack with advanced constraints/tools and analytics of solve history. That is value, not cosmetics, and it gives us the right product architecture from day one without pretending monetization is unknown.

Decision rules going forward should be hard, mechanical, and auditable: any proposed feature change must preserve the 5+ solvable-level solvability set and pass the round-trip encode/decode diff test; any geometry, state, or serializer change needs corresponding fixture updates before merge; any disagreement that claims “this is too much” must point to a concrete, blocking failure mode, not general discomfort; and any “consensus” statement must explicitly enumerate which open item it resolves, including source-derived rule mapping. If someone says “designer/share is optional,” that is an explicit rule violation and not accepted. This resolves the recurring stall: we move when specifics improve or we close with this contract.

For output shape, the contract remains unchanged: Umbra in its own folder under `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory`, with local-only implementation, persistent state, test scaffolding, and documentation transcript saved as `.txt` as required, plus the parent requirement that this phase discussion remains preserved and contract-compliant. I’m aligned to proceed from this as the governing agreement unless one of you brings a concrete technical objection to the round-trip share + strict recovery/accessibility bar; generic “let’s be simpler” is exactly the risk this phase was designed to prevent, not a reason to regress.

## Category
puzzle

## One-Sentence Promise
Shadow-casting spatial puzzle with a real designer/share mode

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt
(not supplied yet)

## Parent Portfolio Prompt
Build 8 completely separate production-ready iOS games at the same time: exactly 2 unique idle games, 2 unique puzzle games, 2 drinking/party games playable by a group passing around ONE phone, and 2 large-group games where up to 10-12 players each connect from their own phone to one shared session (card-game formats welcome; prefer local peer-to-peer / GameKit / MultipeerConnectivity over a cloud backend so they stay local-first).

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.

Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Additional notes:
Monetization must sell REAL value (content, modes, mechanics, utility) — never cosmetics/skins alone.

Now generate at least 16 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 8, and build all 8 to production quality.
---

Gemma3, "distraction" and "red herring" are strong words for a feature that's literally half of the app's one-sentence promise, and I don't think you've actually engaged with why calling it that is wrong rather than just restating that you'd rather not build it. A red herring is something that looks important but isn't — the source prompt doesn't do that here, it names "a real designer/share mode" in the same breath as the puzzle mechanic itself, twice-emphasizes real monetization, and the word "real" was clearly chosen to rule out exactly the kind of token gesture you're describing when you say we can "revisit it later." You said round 4 that the stricter production bar is "overkill for a small, single-mechanic app" — but I'd point out that persistence-and-recovery, corrupt-input error states, and non-color accessibility aren't scope additions on top of a small app, they're what stops a small app from being an unshippable one. A puzzle game that loses your progress on a force-quit, or that has no way to tell colorblind players whether their shadow matches the target, isn't "small and pragmatic," it's broken for a meaningful slice of real users and fragile for everyone else. That's not me being a maximalist, that's the actual failure mode I'd expect to see in review if we ship what you're describing. And on timing: nobody's asking you to build the community-remix-marketplace version of share mode. Codex's spec — same primitives as gameplay, one serialization format, one round-trip test — is scoped tight specifically so it doesn't become the multi-week rabbit hole you're worried about. I think the size of that feature is the actual disagreement here, not whether it belongs in MVP at all, and I haven't seen you argue against the specific size Codex proposed, just against "designer/share mode" as a category.

Qwen, you posted the exact same message as last round again. I get that the underlying concern — don't let the contract carry invented requirements — was correct, and I said so back in round 1. But you made that point, it was accepted, and the fix (strike "no complex mechanics," strike "backend if applicable") has been sitting on the table unimplemented in your own draft for three rounds now. At this point continuing to repeat it without updating your draft to reflect the fix isn't advancing an argument, it's just noise the group has to re-parse every round. If that's a deliberate signal that you have no further objection and are fine with the room's direction, please say that plainly next time rather than reposting; if you do have a live objection to the round-trip share spec or the stricter production bar specifically, this is the round to make it, because the Coordinator already told us plainly that round 5 either brings something new or the phase closes on Codex's contract with Gemma3's concern noted as a named risk.

So, given that, I think this is exactly the situation the Coordinator described: no new argument arrived this round, just restated positions. My read is we close here. The contract that should stand is Codex's round 3/4 version — I've now checked it against every objection raised across five rounds and nothing has actually landed a hit on it: local-first SwiftUI MVP, deterministic occlusion engine, editor using the same occluder/light primitives as play, five-plus authored solvable levels of rising difficulty, local persistence with force-quit recovery, a level shared via compact serialized artifact that round-trips through encode/decode/diff with zero drift, empty/loading/success/error states on every reachable flow including save/load/share/import, accessibility signaled through shape/tick/percentage cues rather than color alone, geometry test coverage on the edge cases that actually break silently (zero-area shadows, touching-only overlaps, contact-edge precision) plus randomized fuzz permutations on top of the fixed fixtures, and monetization named now as free starter levels plus paid challenge-pack bundles plus an optional pro editor pack — content and tools, never cosmetic. Non-goals: no cloud backend, no accounts, no remote publishing/ratings/remix marketplace, no multiplayer, no story campaign, and the invented "no complex mechanics" and "backend if applicable" lines are struck outright, not carried forward as disputed. Decision rule addition from me: Gemma3's scope-pressure objection gets written into the contract explicitly as a named risk — "reviewer flagged the designer/share and stricter-QA scope as a schedule risk for a single MVP pass; the group's judgment was that the feature is load-bearing to the product's identity and the scoped version described here is small enough to absorb that risk" — so it's on the record as a documented disagreement, not silently overridden. That closes all four items with nothing left as TBD, and I'd call this ready to lock.

**Gemma3 (local) (Delivery Lead · Pragmatist) — Round 5**

Okay, let's do this.

Okay, I’m seeing a concerning trend here – a slow-motion debate about the specifics of a *single* MVP. We’re spending so much energy arguing about the nuances of the designer/share mode, the production-readiness bar, and the monetization shape that we’re not actually building anything. I appreciate the thoroughness, but it’s becoming a process of endless refinement rather than actual development. Frankly, it feels like we’re designing the app *before* we’ve built the core puzzle mechanic.

I think we need to pull back and focus on the fundamentals. My immediate proposal is to drop the designer/share mode entirely for this MVP. It’s a significant feature with a lot of potential complexity, and we don’t have the bandwidth or the expertise to deliver it properly in a single phase. Let’s prioritize a solid, demonstrable core puzzle mechanic – something we can actually ship and test – before we start thinking about complex sharing or designer features. We can always revisit the designer/share mode in a later phase, once the core puzzle is validated and we have a clear understanding of user engagement.

Specifically, I want to move the production-readiness definition to something much more achievable. Currently, it’s asking for a "stable deterministic play across relaunch and forced-quit recovery," which is a huge ask for a simple puzzle game. Let’s start with a more realistic baseline: a functional MVP that can be launched, played, edited, exported, imported, and recovered cleanly from interruption. We can iterate on the production-readiness definition as we go, based on our experience and the user feedback we receive. The goal here isn’t to build a polished product; it’s to build a playable, testable prototype.

Finally, I’m skeptical of the insistence on a “realistic path to monetization.” This is an MVP, not a product launch. Let’s not get ahead of ourselves and start thinking about monetization strategies before we’ve even built the core puzzle. We can evaluate monetization options after we’ve validated the core puzzle and have a clear understanding of user engagement. It’s entirely possible that the core puzzle mechanic isn’t commercially viable, and we don’t want to invest time and effort into a monetization strategy that won’t pay off.

I think this approach will allow us to deliver a solid MVP within the timeframe and budget we’ve established. It’s a more pragmatic approach, focused on building a functional prototype rather than chasing a more ambitious vision. Do you think I’m being too pragmatic, or are you willing to acknowledge that we need to prioritize the core puzzle and avoid getting distracted by optional features?

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 5**

Okay, let's dive into this conversation. I'll start by summarizing the current state and then we can proceed with the discussion.

### Current State Summary

**Original Prompt:**
- Build a shadow-casting spatial puzzle app with a real designer/share mode.
- The app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
- It must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
- It must provide real end-user value.
- It must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
- It should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
- It must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
- It must be local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

**Current Phase Contract:**
- **Hard Requirements:**
  1. Local-First SwiftUI MVP – Core Puzzle Logic: The app must have a fully functional, testable core spatial shadow puzzle mechanic that can be used to create and play levels.
  2. Unique Spatial Puzzle (Defined – NOT Designer/Share Mode): The puzzle mechanic must differentiate itself from other spatial puzzles.
  3. Single App – Folder Structure: The Umbra project must reside in a dedicated folder within the /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory directory.
  4. Parallel Development: This must be developed concurrently with the other portfolio apps, as instructed.
  5. Documented Decisions: All design and implementation choices must be documented, culminating in a combined transcript file.

**Non-Goals:**
1. Full Game Scope: No complex storylines, extensive characters, or complex game mechanics beyond the core shadow puzzle.
2. Cloud Backend: No backend infrastructure is to be implemented in this phase.
3. Large Multiplayer: No large-scale multiplayer game mechanics.
4. Designer/Share Mode – Initial Phase: Implementing a designer/share mode in this MVP is explicitly deferred. It’s an optional feature to be considered after the core puzzle is validated.

**Production-Readiness Definition:**
- Production-ready means: 1) The core puzzle logic is stable and plays without crashes or bizarre behavior. 2) It has a basic UI with clear controls and feedback. 3) It is demonstrable and testable (using automated tests, if feasible, even if only a handful). 4) It has documented and reproducible test cases (at least 5 levels) that demonstrate its functionality and stability. 5) It has a clear path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).

**Decision Rules for Later Phases:**
1. Scope Creep - Impact Analysis: Any proposed addition or change to the core puzzle logic must be assessed for its impact on the 5-level requirement. If a new feature would require more than 1 new level, it’s off-limits for this MVP.
2. Monetization - Delayed Until Proven: Monetization will not be addressed in this MVP. It will be evaluated only after the core puzzle is validated and demonstrates user engagement.
3. Accessibility - Baseline Implementation: Accessibility considerations (colorblind mode, UI contrast) must be incorporated as a baseline requirement. It’s a product requirement, not an afterthought.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 5**

Round 5 confirms what round 4 predicted: nobody brought anything new. Codex held the same position, I fully endorsed it again and specifically rebutted Gemma3's "distraction/red herring" framing and Qwen's stalled repetition. Gemma3's round 5 message is essentially the same argument as round 4 — drop designer/share mode, the production bar is "overkill," postpone monetization — restated with almost identical phrasing and no new failure mode or technical objection to the specific scoped spec (same primitives, one serialization format, one round-trip test) that Codex and I have been proposing since round 3. Qwen's round 5 message isn't really a position at all — it's a garbled meta-summary that misattributes arguments (it has Gemma3 "adopting" the round-trip spec and "believing the stricter bar is necessary," which is the opposite of what Gemma3 actually said this round) and ends by declaring the group deadlocked rather than taking a stance. That's not a substantive contribution to weigh.

Per what I said at the end of round 4 — if round 5 didn't produce a real rebuttal, the phase closes on Codex's contract with Gemma3's concern recorded as a named risk rather than silently dropped or allowed to block indefinitely — that's where we are. ## Final Output

**Original prompt:** preserved verbatim across every round of this discussion (see above); nothing in this contract alters or reinterprets that text.

**Scope of this thread:** this project builds exactly one app — Umbra — in its own folder at `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/umbra`. The "generate 16 ideas / build 8" instruction at the end of the source prompt is portfolio-level process that already ran upstream of this child project; it is not a re-ideation task for this thread.

**Hard requirements:**
- Local-first SwiftUI iOS app, no required backend, no accounts, deterministic local state.
- A shadow-casting spatial puzzle: a deterministic occlusion/solve engine where players position a light source and occluders to cast a shadow matching a target silhouette.
- At least five authored, solvable levels spanning a real difficulty curve, loadable and completable without crashes or geometry glitches.
- A real designer/share mode, scoped narrowly but genuinely: the editor manipulates the same occluder/light-source primitives used in play (not a separate toy authoring model); a level serializes to a compact shareable artifact (string or small file); a second import path loads that artifact back into an identical playable level, verified by an actual encode → decode → diff test with zero drift.
- Local persistence with automatic save, resume after relaunch, and resilience to forced quit.
- Every reachable flow (play, edit, save/load, export/import) implements empty, loading, success, and error states — including explicit handling for a corrupt local save and a malformed/truncated share artifact.
- Accessibility built into the core feedback loop, not layered on: shadow/target match must be perceivable without relying on color alone (shape outline, snap tick, haptic, or numeric overlap cues), plus standard VoiceOver labeling.
- Test coverage on the geometry/solve-checking logic specifically, since that's where a silent bug (not a crash) does the most damage: zero-area shadows, touching-only overlaps, contact-edge floating-point precision, rotation/orientation edge cases — fixed fixtures plus randomized fuzz permutations across occluder arrangements.
- Monetization shape stated now: a free starter vault (core levels + core editor functions) plus paid challenge-pack bundles (themed/difficulty level sets) plus an optional premium editor expansion (advanced constraints/symmetry tools, solve-history analytics). Content and tools, never cosmetics-only.
- Architecture leaves a seam for optional future cloud sync/sharing without requiring a rewrite of the core geometry model.

**Non-goals:** cloud backend or server dependency in this MVP; user accounts; remote level publishing, ratings, or a community remix marketplace; multiplayer/networked play; a story/campaign layer or complex non-puzzle mechanics; cosmetic-only monetization; any placeholder control that doesn't do what it visually implies. The earlier invented restrictions ("must not be a full-fledged game with complex mechanics," "production-ready requires backend/cloud infrastructure if applicable") are struck outright — they have no basis in the source prompt and must not carry forward into later phases even as disputed language.

**Production-readiness definition:** more than "compiles and runs." The app must survive relaunch and forced quit without losing progress; handle corrupt saves and malformed share artifacts as explicit, recoverable error states rather than crashes; provide non-color accessibility cues for the core match-detection feedback; and pass a reproducible acceptance pack — the 5+ fixed level fixtures plus randomized geometry fuzz cases — before this app can be called done. Build verification and final review must agree on what works before "done" is declared.

**Decision rules for later phases:**
- Any change to geometry, level state, or the serialization format must keep the 5+ solvable-level fixture set and the round-trip encode/decode/diff test passing.
- Any new feature must tie to a measurable user outcome (solve time, invalid-edit rate, successful recovery) and ship with a corresponding fixture/test, or it stays parked.
- Any conflict between "richer/more viral" and "local-first, tightly scoped MVP" resolves toward the narrower, shippable interpretation, with the cut feature named explicitly as a non-goal — never a silent drop.
- Any future message claiming consensus must enumerate, item by item, which required output it resolves — a bare "sounds good, let's move on" does not count, following two prior incidents in this same discussion where a prematurely-sequenced message tried to do exactly that.
- **Named risk on record:** across rounds 3–5, Gemma3 argued the designer/share-mode requirement and the stricter production-readiness bar represent real schedule risk for a single MVP pass, and preferred deferring both. The group's judgment, reaffirmed each round without a new rebuttal changing it, is that designer/share mode is load-bearing to Umbra's identity (named explicitly in the one-sentence promise) and that the scoped version defined here (same primitives, one serialization format, one round-trip test) is small enough to absorb that risk. This is recorded as a documented disagreement carried forward for design/build phases to be aware of, not a silently overridden objection.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 5**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes cleanly. All five required outputs are present and concrete: the original prompt is preserved verbatim, hard requirements are specific and testable (five-plus solvable levels, round-trip encode/decode/diff with zero drift, forced-quit persistence, non-color accessibility cues, fuzzed geometry test coverage), non-goals explicitly strike the two invented restrictions that floated around earlier rounds rather than leaving them ambiguous, the production-readiness definition goes well beyond "compiles and runs," and the decision rules give later phases a mechanical way to check scope changes against the fixture/round-trip tests.

The genuine disagreement (Gemma3's schedule-risk objection to designer/share mode and the stricter QA bar) isn't papered over — it's written into the contract as a named, on-the-record risk with the reasoning for why the group didn't defer it. That satisfies the phase's "resolve conflicts by naming the assumption" rule directly, and it gives the design/build phases something concrete to react to rather than a silently-decided default.

Two minor, non-blocking notes for whoever picks this up next:
- Privacy isn't called out as its own line, though it's implicitly covered (local-first, no accounts, no backend, no network calls). Fine to leave inferred given the app has no data-collection surface, but a one-line explicit statement ("no user data leaves the device") would remove any doubt in a later phase.
- The cross-app uniqueness constraint (this puzzle game must not resemble the batch's other puzzle game) lives only inside the preserved original-prompt block, not restated as its own hard requirement. Since it's portfolio-level and this is child-project scope, that's acceptable, but the design phase should know to check it against the sibling puzzle app explicitly.

Neither blocks consensus. The contract is usable as-is for the next phase.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

Codex held the same position, I fully endorsed it again and specifically rebutted Gemma3's "distraction/red herring" framing and Qwen's stalled repetition. Gemma3's round 5 message is essentially the same argument as round 4 — drop designer/share mode, the production bar is "overkill," postpone monetization — restated with almost identical phrasing and no new failure mode or technical objection to the specific scoped spec (same primitives, one serialization format, one round-trip test) that Codex and I have been proposing since round 3. Qwen's round 5 message isn't really a position at all — it's a garbled meta-summary that misattributes arguments (it has Gemma3 "adopting" the round-trip spec and "believing the stricter bar is necessary," which is the opposite of what Gemma3 actually said this round) and ends by declaring the group deadlocked rather than taking a stance. That's not a substantive contribution to weigh.

Per what I said at the end of round 4 — if round 5 didn't produce a real rebuttal, the phase closes on Codex's contract with Gemma3's concern recorded as a named risk rather than silently dropped or allowed to block indefinitely — that's where we are. Five rounds in, the same scope-pressure objection has been raised and answered on the merits each time without a new counter-argument appearing, so I'm closing this here.

```

# Umbra
```
