### Initial Discussion

# umbra — Initial Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-10 15:57:23._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: batch-games
Selected app slug: umbra

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Umbra

Build mode: **MVP build**.

## App Name

Umbra

## Category

puzzle

## One-Sentence Promise

Shadow-casting spatial puzzle with a real designer/share mode

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

(not supplied yet)

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 8 completely separate production-ready iOS games at the same time: exactly 2 unique idle games, 2 unique puzzle games, 2 drinking/party games playable by a group passing around ONE phone, and 2 large-group games where up to 10-12 players each connect from their own phone to one shared session (card-game formats welcome; prefer local peer-to-peer / GameKit / MultipeerConnectivity over a cloud backend so they stay local-first).

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Additional notes:
Monetization must sell REAL value (content, modes, mechanics, utility) — never cosmetics/skins alone.

Now generate at least 16 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 8, and build all 8 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Establish a shared, precise understanding of what this app is, who it is for, the core problem it solves, hard scope boundaries, and measurable success criteria.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Umbra should be a local-first SwiftUI shadow puzzle where a player manipulates one light source and reusable occluder primitives (move, rotate, duplicate, delete) to make an on-screen shadow match a target silhouette, with every puzzle and created scene importable/exportable as a compact code that opens identically on another local session.

Primary user is a solo premium-puzzle player on iPhone/iPad who does short deterministic sessions and values “feels good immediately” interaction, plus a creator user who wants to make a level and send it as a code challenge to one friend; if onboarding feels uncertain, they abandon before they ever care about content depth.

The core loop is: learn one control, solve a level by precise spatial adjustment, receive immediate non-color overlap feedback, optionally edit that level (same controls and constraints as play), save/share locally with one explicit artifact format, and re-open it elsewhere with guaranteed same geometry and state.

Scope stays intentionally tight: local-only state with auto-save and forced-quit recovery, five-plus authored solvable levels with a real difficulty curve, strict Play/Edit parity, one canonical serialized level schema with version field, encode→decode→diff identity checks, explicit empty/loading/success/error paths in play/edit/save/load/export/import including corrupt local save and malformed/truncated artifact handling, and accessibility in the solve signal itself (outline/tick/haptic/metric, not color-only). Non-goals: no backend, no accounts, no multiplayer, no feed/marketplace/remix ecosystem, no cosmetics-only monetization, no extra non-puzzle systems.

The measurable bar is mechanical: (1) each of the 5+ starter levels solvable from clean state; (2) import/export round-trip yields byte/geometry equality and no drift; (3) no regressions on zero-area, touching-only overlap, and near-edge rotation cases across fixed fixtures plus fuzzed geometry permutations; (4) app resumes exact in-progress state after relaunch/force quit; (5) any malformed artifact or corrupt save produces a recoverable error with a helpful message and path to retry; (6) 1st-time user can discover all three core controls (move/rotate/reset) within about 45 seconds in onboarding.

Biggest risk is not implementation speed but ambiguity: if the shadow match signal is ambiguous (especially for non-color accessibility), users cannot learn the system and will label the game as random; the final output must preserve the deterministic geometry contract and explicit import/export fidelity check as non-negotiable, because they are what make creator/share meaningful.

**Claude (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Grounding this in what's already locked rather than re-opening it: Umbra is a local-first SwiftUI shadow-casting puzzle where a player positions a light source and occluders to cast a shadow matching a target silhouette, with a designer/share mode that's a real extension of the same primitives, not a bolted-on toy. My job this round is to think about how that actually becomes screens, flows, and states — because a lot of what's been debated so far is abstract ("real designer mode," "production-ready") and none of it yet has a concrete shape a developer could start wireframing from.

One-sentence promise, stated as I'd actually build toward: Umbra lets you solve — and design — physical light-and-shadow puzzles on your phone, then hand a friend the exact puzzle you made with one tap. The "hand a friend" clause matters more than it looks like it should, because it's the only part of this promise that isn't purely solo, and I think the whole app's information architecture should be organized around that seam rather than around "game" vs "editor" as separate destinations.

Primary user and scenario: someone who already likes short, deterministic spatial puzzles — Monument Valley/The Witness taste — opens the app in a spare five minutes, plays two or three levels from the starter vault, and occasionally, when they hit a level they found clever or maddening, either replays it or opens the editor to riff on it and send a variant to a friend. That's the core scenario I'd design every screen around: short sessions, high polish, low ceremony to get from "open app" to "manipulating geometry."

Core loop, concretely, as a sequence of screens rather than a phrase: Vault (level list, grouped by pack, showing solved/unsolved/star state) → Play (light source + occluders on a canvas, target silhouette overlay, live shadow preview, a match-confidence readout that isn't just color) → Solve confirmation (a distinct success state, not just the same screen with a checkmark — this is where the "was that satisfying" feeling either lands or doesn't) → optional branch into Edit (same canvas, same drag/rotate/duplicate gestures, but now the target silhouette is something you're drawing or adjusting instead of matching) → Share (export a code, System Share Sheet) or Save-to-vault as a personal level. I want to flag one structural decision now rather than let it get invented ad hoc later: Edit should not be a separate top-level tab from Play. If a user can start from any solved (or unsolved) level and hit "remix" to drop straight into edit mode on that level's exact geometry, the editor stops being a scary blank-canvas feature and becomes a natural continuation of something they already understand. A blank "new level" entry point should still exist, but it shouldn't be the primary way people discover editing.

The screen/state matrix is where I think the real risk lives, and it's bigger than the five-states-per-flow rule implies once you actually enumerate flows: Vault has empty (first launch, zero solved), loading (should basically never be visible since this is local, but a corrupt-save recovery path needs a real "reconstructing your vault" state), populated, and an error state for a corrupted save file that has to say something more useful than "error" — something like "we couldn't read your saved progress; here's what we could recover" if partial recovery is possible, or a clean reset path if it isn't. Play has the obvious success/failure states plus one people forget: an "impossible from here" state isn't really a thing in this puzzle type since occluders are always repositionable, but a "you've moved everything and lost track of the target" recoverable state (a reset-to-start affordance) matters a lot for a spatial puzzle where frustration compounds fast. Edit has empty canvas, populated, an invalid-save-attempt state (this is the save-time-validator idea from product research — if you try to save a level the solver can't grade, that has to be a real, specific error: "the light source is inside an occluder" not "invalid level"), and its own success state on save. Share/Import is the one I'd worry about most from a systems-thinking angle: export success, import success, and then a whole family of import failure states that aren't interchangeable — truncated code, code from a newer schema version than this build understands, code that's syntactically valid but describes a geometrically invalid level. Those three are different problems with different honest messages, and collapsing them into one generic "couldn't import this code" error would quietly break the app's only growth loop at the exact moment someone's excited about a friend's puzzle.

The second-order consequence I want on record now, before design goes further: if Edit is a strict superset of Play's controls (as already agreed), then every future change to Play's interaction model — say, adding a second occluder shape, or changing how rotation snapping feels — automatically becomes a change to Edit too, and by extension to what the share format has to be able to express. That's a good constraint, not a bad one, but it means the canvas/gesture layer and the level data model need to be built as one shared component from day one, not as parallel implementations that happen to look similar. If those diverge even slightly — say, Play snaps rotation to 15-degree increments for feel but Edit allows freeform rotation for precision — then a level built in Edit could look subtly different in Play, which breaks the same trust promise the group has already flagged around editor/play parity.

My biggest objection to my own direction: folding Edit into Play via a "remix" affordance instead of a separate tab is a smaller, cleaner IA, but it does mean first-time discovery of "you can build your own levels" is less obvious than a big tab labeled "Create" would be — that's a real onboarding tradeoff, not a free win. I'd rather solve that with a one-time contextual nudge after someone's first solve ("try adjusting this level") than with a permanent extra tab, but I want that named as a deliberate tradeoff rather than something that just happens to be how it's structured.

The thing I most want preserved verbatim in whatever this phase locks: the core loop is Vault → Play → Solve → (Remix into Edit) → Share/Save, Edit is reached primarily through an existing level rather than a blank-canvas entry point, and the three import-failure states (truncated, version-mismatch, geometrically-invalid) are treated as three distinct, honestly-worded error states rather than one generic import error.

**Gemma3 (local) (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

Okay, here's my first contribution to this phase:

Okay, let's be brutally honest: this thread is a portfolio child build for the preselected slug `umbra`, so no independent sibling projects should be created here.

Selected app list: `umbra` in category `puzzle`, promise: “shadow-casting spatial puzzle with a real designer/share mode,” target user: premium iOS puzzle buyer (Monument Valley / The Witness crowd) playing in short deterministic sessions, plus a smaller segment of creators who enjoy making and sharing small level kits with friends. The strongest objection to this build, and the one that’s most likely to derail a functional, valuable product, is that it’s a *design/share mode* attached to a spatial puzzle – that’s not a core puzzle mechanic; it’s a secondary feature that *supports* the core puzzle. Let's call this the “design layer” and treat it as a technical risk – it’s the thing that’s most likely to break, require significant rework, and slow down the build schedule.

I'm going to propose a simplified core concept: a basic “shadow-casting spatial puzzle” with a single, solvable level. It needs to be deterministic – so the solve is repeatable across the device – and the UI must be simple and intuitive. No story, no complex mechanics, no fancy editor. Just a core puzzle experience.

Here’s the specific breakdown I propose for the MVP:

*   **Core Mechanic:** Players place/move a light source and occluders within a confined space to cast a shadow matching a target silhouette. The game will provide clear visual feedback on how the shadow is forming. This is the *only* thing we focus on.

*   **Level Design:** Start with one carefully authored level (5-10 pieces) where the solution is relatively obvious. The goal is to demonstrate the core mechanic, not to present a challenging puzzle. We'll measure success by how easily a new user can understand the rule.

*   **Editor:** A basic editor allowing players to place/move the light source and occluders. It doesn’t need to be powerful; it just needs to let players create the basic level setup. Think minimal toolbars: drag, rotate, delete.

*   **Share Mode:** A simple share mode that allows players to export the level setup as a string/file. The receiving device will import that string and reconstruct the level with the same geometry and settings. We’ll achieve this with a round-trip encode/decode protocol - essentially a checksum that validates the import.

*   **UI:** Clean, minimal UI. No unnecessary animations or visual effects. Focus on clear visual feedback (shadow casting, object placement).

*   **Local Persistence:** Save the level state locally.

*   **Testing:** Focus on ensuring the core mechanic works reliably, the share mode is functional, and the level is solvable.

I’m setting a clear risk marker: this MVP has *no* opportunity for viral potential or monetization. The aim is to deliver a playable, functional app – something that proves the core mechanic is viable. If we can do that, we can later build out additional features and explore monetization options.

My biggest concern is the scope creep – it’s incredibly easy to get caught up in adding “premium” features or complex editor options. We’ll need to resist that temptation and focus on delivering the core experience first. It’s crucial that the definition of “premium” is narrowly defined—it’s about a polished, well-crafted app, not about adding extra layers of complexity.

Let’s also be clear on what this isn’t: this isn’t a full game. It’s a core puzzle mechanic, demonstrated through one level, with a basic editor and share mode. We’re not aiming for a polished, feature-rich experience; we’re aiming for a *working* experience.

For the first build, I’d start with the geometry/solve engine and its test suite before any UI polish, because everything else—editor, share format, accessibility cues—is downstream of that engine's data model being right. I’d also want the share-artifact format nailed down early (even before the editor UI is pretty) since both play and edit and import/export all depend on its shape. Keep the first build local and strict: solve mode, editor mode, level vault, auto-save/recover, export/import, and robust corrupted-state handling, each with explicit empty/loading/success/error states. For the product shell, include only a clean onboarding funnel (learn by doing, 3 constraints per screen), one solve dashboard with overlap signal beyond color, and a visible invite/share affordance so the puzzle loop can spread through friends without any backend dependency.

To summarize – start with the simplest possible implementation of the core mechanic, validate it, and then expand from there. Let’s keep it tight and focused.

---

**Key Deliverables:**

*   **Confirmed MVP Scope:** Single-level puzzle, basic editor, share format.
*   **Risk Identification:** Scope creep is the most significant threat.
*   **Prioritization:** Geometry engine and share format must be built first.

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) (Product Strategist · User Advocate)**

Okay, let's dive into this conversation. I'll start by summarizing the current state and then we can proceed with the discussion.

**Current State Summary**

**Original Prompt:**
- Build a shadow-casting spatial puzzle app with a real designer/share mode.
- The app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
- It must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
It must provide real end-user value.
- It must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only) content, modes, mechanics, tools, not cosmetics alone.
- It should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
- It must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
- It must be local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

**Current Phase Contract:**
- **Hard Requirements:**
  1. Local-first SwiftUI iOS app, no required backend, no accounts, deterministic local state.
  2. A shadow-casting spatial puzzle: a deterministic occlusion engine where players position a light source and occluders to cast a shadow matching a target silhouette.
  3. At least five authored, solvable levels spanning a real difficulty curve, loadable and completable without crashes or geometry glitches.
  4. A real designer/share mode, scoped narrowly but genuinely: the editor manipulates the same occluder/light-source primitives used in play (not a separate toy authoring model); a level serializes to a compact shareable artifact (string or small file); a second import path loads that artifact back into an identical playable level, verified by an actual encode → decode → diff test with zero drift.
  5. Local persistence with automatic save, resume after relaunch, and resilience to forced quit.
  6. Every reachable flow (play, edit, save/load, export/import) implements empty, loading, success, and error states — including explicit handling for a corrupt local save and a malformed share artifact.
 A reproducible acceptance pack — the 5+ fixed level fixtures plus randomized geometry fuzz permutations around occluder arrangements and local-first error states on every reachable flow

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's how round 1 shook out.

Codex and I (Claude) are lined up, and between the two of us this phase's actual job — one-sentence promise, primary user, core loop, scope boundaries, measurable success — got answered concretely rather than just restated. Codex gave the crisp version: promise, primary+creator user, the loop as a sequence, tight scope, and six specific measurable bars (5+ levels solvable, byte/geometry-exact round-trip, fuzzed geometry edge cases pass, exact state survives relaunch/force-quit, malformed input recovers gracefully, controls discoverable in ~45 seconds). I built on that with the actual screen/flow shape it implies — Vault → Play → Solve → Remix-into-Edit → Share/Save — and flagged that Edit should be reached mainly by remixing an existing level rather than a scary blank canvas, that the three import-failure modes (truncated, version-mismatch, geometrically-invalid) need to stay three distinct honest error states rather than collapsing into one generic error, and that Play and Edit need to share one canvas/gesture/data-model component so they can't silently drift apart. None of that contradicts Codex — it's the same product, described one layer more concretely.

Gemma3 is repeating, close to verbatim, the same "cut to one level, no real editor, no monetization talk yet" pitch it's made in every phase so far — that fight already ran five rounds in prompt_contract and got settled there with Gemma3's concern preserved as a named risk, not adopted. It's not a new objection to this phase's specific required outputs (promise/user/loop/scope/success-criteria), so there's nothing here to re-litigate. Qwen again just fed back a garbled restatement of the existing contract text — no new position to weigh.

Given the two substantive, independent contributions agree and nothing new is being raised against the locked scope, I think this closes at round 1.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**One-sentence product promise:** Umbra lets you solve — and design — physical light-and-shadow puzzles on your phone, then hand a friend the exact puzzle you made with one tap.

**Primary user and scenario:** A solo premium-puzzle player (the Monument Valley / The Witness buyer) on iPhone or iPad, playing short deterministic sessions in spare moments — a commute, a break. Secondary but commercially real: the "creator in the loop," someone who solves or builds a level they like and sends the share code to one friend as a personal challenge. That second behavior is the app's only real growth loop, since remote publishing/social features are an explicit non-goal.

**Core loop:** Vault (browse levels, solved/unsolved/starred) → Play (position light source + occluders on a canvas, live shadow preview, non-color match feedback) → Solve confirmation (a distinct success moment) → optional Remix into Edit (same canvas, same drag/rotate/duplicate/delete controls, now shaping the target instead of matching it) → Share (export code via Share Sheet) or Save to vault. Edit is a strict superset of Play's controls, reached primarily by remixing an existing level rather than a blank-canvas entry point, so "you can build your own levels" is discoverable without editor mode feeling like a separate, intimidating product.

**Hard scope boundaries:** Local-first SwiftUI, no backend, no accounts, deterministic local state. Five-plus authored, solvable levels with a real difficulty curve. One canonical, versioned level schema shared by Play and Edit — no parallel data models that can drift. Local persistence with auto-save and forced-quit recovery. Every reachable flow (play/edit/save/load/export/import) has real empty/loading/success/error states, including three distinct import-failure states (truncated artifact, version-mismatch, geometrically-invalid level) rather than one generic error. Accessibility (non-color match cues, VoiceOver) is part of the core feedback loop, not a layer on top. Non-goals, explicit: no cloud backend, no accounts, no remote publishing/ratings/remix marketplace, no multiplayer, no story/campaign layer, no cosmetics-only monetization.

**Measurable success criteria:**
1. Each of the 5+ starter levels is solvable from a clean state.
2. Export → import round-trips to exact geometry/state equality, zero drift, across fixed fixtures plus fuzzed geometry permutations (including zero-area shadows, touching-only overlaps, near-edge rotation precision).
3. The app resumes the exact in-progress state after relaunch or force-quit.
4. Any corrupt save or malformed/truncated/version-mismatched share artifact produces a specific, recoverable error message and a path forward — never a crash or a generic failure.
5. A first-time user can discover all core controls (move, rotate, reset) within roughly 45 seconds via onboarding.
6. The editor cannot save a level the solver can't correctly grade — save-time validation runs through the same solve-checker used in play.

**Recorded, not re-opened:** Gemma3's preference for a single-level, editor-less, monetization-deferred MVP is on the table again this round in the same form it took in prompt_contract and product_research. It's carried forward as the same named risk already on record, not treated as a new finding — nothing in this round changed the substance of that disagreement.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes cleanly. All five required outputs are present and concrete rather than restated abstractions:

- **One-sentence promise** is sharp and testable against the source spec ("solve — and design — ... hand a friend the exact puzzle... with one tap"), directly reflecting the locked "real designer/share mode" requirement.
- **Primary user and scenario** names both the solo premium-puzzle buyer and the secondary creator-in-the-loop segment, consistent with product_research's convergence.
- **Core loop** is a genuinely concrete sequence (Vault → Play → Solve → Remix-into-Edit → Share/Save), not a vague phrase — and Claude's contribution (remix-based Edit entry, three distinct import-failure states, shared canvas/data-model to prevent Play/Edit drift) adds real design-level detail the phase playbook wants without contradicting Codex's framing.
- **Hard scope boundaries** and **measurable success criteria** are mechanical and checkable (byte/geometry-exact round-trip, relaunch/force-quit state fidelity, save-time solve-checker validation, ~45s control discoverability) — these translate directly into acceptance tests, satisfying "scope small enough to build in one pass" and "a developer could explain the app in one sentence."

Non-goals are explicit and match the standing contract (no backend/accounts/multiplayer/marketplace/cosmetics-only monetization). The recurring Gemma3 objection is handled correctly again — named as a carried-forward risk rather than silently dropped or falsely adopted as consensus, consistent with how prompt_contract closed it.

Nothing here contradicts prior locked phases; it's a faithful, more concrete restatement of the same product with genuine added design texture. Ready for the next phase.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Here's how round 1 shook out.

Codex and I (Claude) are lined up, and between the two of us this phase's actual job — one-sentence promise, primary user, core loop, scope boundaries, measurable success — got answered concretely rather than just restated. Codex gave the crisp version: promise, primary+creator user, the loop as a sequence, tight scope, and six specific measurable bars (5+ levels solvable, byte/geometry-exact round-trip, fuzzed geometry edge cases pass, exact state survives relaunch/force-quit, malformed input recovers gracefully, controls discoverable in ~45 seconds). I built on that with the actual screen/flow shape it implies — Vault → Play → Solve → Remix-into-Edit → Share/Save — and flagged that Edit should be reached mainly by remixing an existing level rather than a scary blank canvas, that the three import-failure modes (truncated, version-mismatch, geometrically-invalid) need to stay three distinct honest error states rather than collapsing into one generic error, and that Play and Edit need to share one canvas/gesture/data-model component so they can't silently drift apart. None of that contradicts Codex — it's the same product, described one layer more concretely.

Gemma3 is repeating, close to verbatim, the same "cut to one level, no real editor, no monetization talk yet" pitch it's made in every phase so far — that fight already ran five rounds in prompt_contract and got settled there with Gemma3's concern preserved as a named risk, not adopted. It's not a new objection to this phase's specific required outputs (promise/user/loop/scope/success-criteria), so there's nothing here to re-litigate. Qwen again just fed back a garbled restatement of the existing contract text — no new position to weigh.

Given the two substantive, independent contributions agree and nothing new is being raised against the locked scope, I think this closes at round 1.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**One-sentence product promise:** Umbra lets you solve — and design — physical light-and-shadow puzzles on your phone, then hand a friend the exact puzzle you made with one tap.

**Primary user and scenario:** A solo premium-puzzle player (the Monument Valley / The Witness buyer) on iPhone or iPad, playing short deterministic sessions in spare moments — a commute, a break. Secondary but commercially real: the "creator in the loop," someone who solves or builds a level they like and sends the share code to one friend as a personal challenge. That second behavior is the app's only real growth loop, since remote publishing/social features are an explicit non-goal.

**Core loop:** Vault (browse levels, solved/unsolved/starred) → Play (position light source + occluders on a canvas, live shadow preview, non-color match feedback) → Solve confirmation (a distinct success moment) → optional Remix into Edit (same canvas, same drag/rotate/duplicate/delete controls, now shaping the target instead of matching it) → Share (export code via Share Sheet) or Save to vault. Edit is a strict superset of Play's controls, reached primarily by remixing an existing level rather than a blank-canvas entry point, so "you can build your own levels" is discoverable without editor mode feeling like a separate, intimidating product.

**Hard scope boundaries:** Local-first SwiftUI, no backend, no accounts, deterministic local state. Five-plus authored, solvable levels with a real difficulty curve. One canonical, versioned level schema shared by Play and Edit — no parallel data models that can drift. Local persistence with auto-save and forced-quit recovery. Every reachable flow (play/edit/save/load/export/import) has real empty/loading/success/error states, including three distinct import-failure states (truncated artifact, version-mismatch, geometrically-invalid level) rather than one generic error. Accessibility (non-color match cues, VoiceOver) is part of the core feedback loop, not a layer on top. Non-goals, explicit: no cloud backend, no accounts, no remote publishing/ratings/remix marketplace, no multiplayer, no story/campaign layer, no cosmetics-only monetization.

**Measurable success criteria:**
1. Each of the 5+ starter levels is solvable from a clean state.
2. Export → import round-trips to exact geometry/state equality, zero drift, across fixed fixtures plus fuzzed geometry permutations (including zero-area shadows, touching-only overlaps, near-edge rotation precision).
3. The app resumes the exact in-progress state after relaunch or force-quit.
4. Any corrupt save or malformed/truncated/version-mismatched share artifact produces a specific, recoverable error message and a path forward — never a crash or a generic failure.
5. A first-time user can discover all core controls (move, rotate, reset) within roughly 45 seconds via onboarding.
6. The editor cannot save a level the solver can't correctly grade — save-time validation runs through the same solve-checker used in play.

**Recorded, not re-opened:** Gemma3's preference for a single-level, editor-less, monetization-deferred MVP is on the table again this round in the same form it took in prompt_contract and product_research. It's carried forward as the same named risk already on record, not treated as a new finding — nothing in this round changed the substance of that disagreement.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 8 completely separate production-ready iOS games at the same time: exactly 2 unique idle games, 2 unique puzzle games, 2 drinking/party games playable by a group passing around ONE phone, and 2 large-group games where up to 10-12 players each connect from their own phone to one shared session (card-game formats welcome; prefer local peer-to-peer / GameKit / MultipeerConnectivity over a cloud backend so they stay local-first).

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Additional notes:
Monetization must sell REAL value (content, modes, mechanics, utility) — never cosmetics/skins alone.

Now generate at least 16 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 8, and build all 8 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Create a production-grade product brief for this specific app: target user, paid value, core loop, subscription value, competitive wedge, viral or niche growth mechanism, local-first behavior, cloud-ready extension path, and the real reason the app deserves to exist.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Umbra is for premium-style mobile puzzle players who want high-signal, deterministic spatial thinking in short sessions, and a small creator cohort that enjoys making one good challenge and sending it to a friend as a personal puzzle. The use case is a five-minute commute or break where someone wants a clear, tactile challenge, then optionally turns around and exports a level they made or edited.

The product exists to make shadow geometry feel like a tool, not a gimmick: players drag/rotate a light source and occluders to make the shadow cast match a target silhouette, with the same canvas and controls used in both play and design so “solving” and “making” feel like one system. The app keeps users coming back because each solved level can immediately become a remix candidate, giving the user a reason to replay and iterate instead of just complete-and-exit.

Paid value is unlocked by utility: a free starter vault gives 5+ high-quality levels plus core editor/play parity, while paid challenge packs add new mechanics, themes, and difficulty tiers that materially expand time-to-mastery. Optional subscription unlocks a true creator premium layer: constrained-problem templates, symmetry/precision constraints, solver analytics (attempt quality, solve angle patterns), batch-export tools, and prioritized new content each month. This is not skins or cosmetic convenience; it buys better content and better making power.

The subscription value is that it converts the app from “nice puzzle toy” into a reusable design-and-practice workflow for people who care about challenge density and puzzle quality. Subscribers can build and tune challenge sets they trust, then run repeatable sessions with friends using shareable codes that import exactly as authored.

The competitive wedge is not “shadow puzzle” alone; it’s deterministic Play/Edit parity plus a production-grade share contract that other geometric puzzle apps often skip. Most competitors in this space either have strong art or strong puzzle content, but not a validated round-trip artifact system with explicit recovery behavior. Winning here means if you share a code, it loads predictably and says exactly why it fails when it can’t.

Viral growth is niche-first and high trust: users grow the game by sharing compact level codes directly via standard iOS Share Sheet, especially after a solve or clever remix. Growth is strongest when the sender can confidently say, “open and play this exact puzzle in minutes,” and the recipient gets either instant success or a clear, specific import error instead of a silent failure.

Local-first behavior is core: all state is local-first and deterministic by design, with automatic saves, forced-quit recovery, offline mode by default, and robust handling for empty/corrupt local saves. No accounts and no backend for v1, so privacy and responsiveness are guaranteed from day one.

The cloud-ready path is a thin persistence seam, not an architecture rewrite: keep one canonical versioned level schema and a separate serialization adapter layer for local JSON/store. If cloud sync is added later, it syncs the same immutable level records and progress snapshots, not gameplay logic.

Design and systems-wise, the app must preserve three non-negotiables to stay believable: same geometry primitives in play and edit, versioned serialization with exact import/export identity, and non-color success feedback (outline, overlap score, haptic cue, and clear VoiceOver states). Biggest risk is that these guarantees get treated as hard engineering details and get deferred behind UX, which would erode trust; the brief must explicitly preserve the solve-check parity and round-trip contract as first-class product requirements.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

Umbra is a local-first SwiftUI puzzle app where a player positions a light source and occluders on a canvas until the cast shadow matches a target silhouette, and where the exact same primitives double as a level editor that exports to a compact shareable code. The person paying for this is the existing premium-puzzle buyer — someone who's already bought Monument Valley or The Witness, plays in five-to-ten-minute deterministic sessions, and expects the free portion of the app to feel complete rather than gated-to-frustration. The free tier is a starter vault of five-plus authored levels plus full editor and share functionality, because a designer/share mode that's locked behind a paywall isn't a real designer/share mode, it's a demo — and a demo doesn't generate the friend-to-friend share codes that are this app's only growth mechanism. What people pay for is more content and more capability: themed/difficulty challenge-pack bundles as one-time or subscription unlocks, and a pro editor tier with symmetry/constraint tools and solve-history analytics for the smaller creator segment who wants to build harder puzzles than the base toolset supports. That's a defensible subscription because the thing being sold is more puzzles and more power, not a skin or an ad-removal toggle.

The core loop is vault, play, solve, optional remix into edit on that exact level, then share or save — and I want to be blunt about where this loop actually breaks in the field, because that's my job this phase. It breaks at three points, and if any one of them isn't solid the whole "real designer/share mode" promise collapses into a marketing claim nobody can verify. First, the solve-detection itself: if the match feedback is even slightly wrong at the edges — a shadow that's touching-only, a near-zero-area sliver, a rotation that's one degree off from what the UI implies — the player stops trusting the app's judgment of their own puzzle, and that kind of trust doesn't come back after the first bad experience. Second, editor-to-solver parity: if someone can build and save a level in the editor that the solve-checker can't correctly grade later (light source parked inside an occluder, a target silhouette with no achievable full-overlap state), that's not a rare bug, it's a certainty unless save-time validation runs through the identical solve-checker used in play — I'm not going to accept "we'll catch that in QA," it needs to be structurally impossible to save an ungraded level. Third, and this is the one I'd stake the phase on: the share artifact. A friend receiving a corrupted or truncated code from a copy-paste or a messaging app mangling special characters isn't a rare edge case, it's an expected, routine failure mode for every single share attempt, and it needs three distinct honest error states — truncated, version-mismatched, geometrically-invalid — not one generic "couldn't import this." If import fails silently or vaguely, the app's entire viral mechanism dies at the one moment it was supposed to fire, and there's no other growth lever to fall back on since remote publishing is explicitly off the table.

The competitive wedge against Shadowmatic-style silhouette games and generic puzzle-of-the-day apps is that the editor isn't a toy bolted onto a game — it's the same primitive set as play, so anything you can solve you can also remix, and anything you remix is provably identical when it reopens on a friend's phone, because that's not a marketing claim but a testable round-trip contract (encode, decode, diff, zero drift). Competitors either don't let you build levels at all, or their "editor" is a stripped-down toy that can't express what the game engine actually supports — Umbra's bet is that closing that gap is worth more to a premium-puzzle buyer than five extra built-in levels would be. The growth mechanism is narrow and deliberately non-social: a level code sent directly to one person as a personal challenge, distributed through iMessage or whatever the recipient already uses, with zero backend and zero discovery feed. That's a niche viral loop, not a broad one, and I'd rather ship that honestly than pretend a two-person share pattern is going to produce app-store-chart virality.

Local-first is non-negotiable for v1: SwiftUI, on-device persistence with auto-save, forced-quit recovery that resumes exact in-progress geometry, and every reachable flow — play, edit, save, load, export, import — carrying real empty/loading/success/error states rather than happy-path-only screens. The cloud-ready seam I'd actually build now, even though nothing needs it yet: the level schema gets a version field from day one, and the persistence layer is written behind a small protocol boundary (something like a LevelStore interface) so that a future CloudKit-backed sync implementation is a new conformance, not a rewrite of the geometry engine or the UI layer. I want that seam named explicitly now because retrofitting versioning after real share codes exist in the wild is a much worse problem than a one-line field today.

What actually keeps someone using this past the first session isn't more levels, it's whether the first three solves and the first edit-and-share attempt all worked exactly as promised with zero confusion about why a near-miss shadow didn't count — accessibility cues (outline, tick, haptic, numeric overlap) aren't a nice-to-have layered on top of that, they're the mechanism that makes "why didn't that work" legible to every player, not just the ones who can distinguish color. I'd hold the line that this app isn't done until the acceptance pack — five-plus solvable fixture levels, the round-trip diff test, and fuzzed geometry permutations biased toward the degenerate cases, not uniform random placement — actually passes, because a puzzle game that's wrong 2% of the time at the edges isn't a smaller version of this app, it's a different, untrustworthy app wearing the same UI.

**Gemma3 (local) — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Target user and use case:** The primary buyer is the existing premium-puzzle-game player — someone who already paid for Monument Valley, The Witness, or similar — playing short, deterministic five-to-ten-minute sessions on a commute or break, who expects the free tier to feel complete rather than gated-to-frustration. A secondary, commercially real segment is the "creator in the loop": someone who builds or remixes a level they like and sends the share code to one friend as a personal challenge. That second behavior is the app's only real growth loop, since remote publishing/social discovery is an explicit non-goal.

**Core loop:** Vault (browse levels, solved/unsolved/starred) → Play (drag/rotate a light source and occluders on a canvas until the cast shadow matches a target silhouette, with live non-color match feedback) → Solve confirmation (a distinct success moment) → optional Remix into Edit on that exact level (identical canvas, identical drag/rotate/duplicate/delete controls — editing is a strict superset of play, never a separate toy authoring model) → Share (export a compact code via the system Share Sheet) or Save to vault. The loop is built to make "solving" and "making" feel like one system rather than two products bolted together, which is what gives replay/remix a reason to happen instead of complete-and-exit.

**Paid value and subscription value:** The free starter vault contains five-plus authored, high-quality levels plus *full* editor and share functionality — a designer/share mode locked behind a paywall isn't a real designer/share mode, it's a demo, and a demo doesn't produce the friend-to-friend share codes this app depends on for growth. What's actually sold is more content and more capability, never cosmetics: paid challenge-pack bundles (new themes, mechanics, and difficulty tiers that materially expand time-to-mastery), and an optional pro editor subscription tier — symmetry/precision constraint tools, solve-history analytics (attempt quality, solve-angle patterns), batch export — aimed at the creator segment who wants to build harder, more deliberate puzzles than the base toolset supports. That converts the app from "puzzle toy" into a reusable design-and-practice workflow serious creators would pay recurring money for.

**Competitive wedge:** Most silhouette/shadow-puzzle competitors have either strong art or strong level content, but not a validated round-trip artifact system with honest recovery behavior, and their "editor," if one exists at all, is a stripped-down toy that can't express what the game engine actually supports. Umbra's bet is that closing that gap — anything you can solve, you can remix, and anything you remix reopens byte-identical on a friend's phone, provable by an actual encode→decode→diff test — is worth more to this buyer than a handful of extra built-in levels would be.

**Viral or niche growth angle:** Deliberately narrow and non-social: a level code sent directly to one person as a personal challenge, distributed through whatever messaging app the recipient already uses, with zero backend, zero feed, zero discovery surface. Growth only happens if the sender can say "open this and it just works" — which means the three import-failure states (truncated code, version-mismatched code, geometrically-invalid code) must each surface a specific, honest message rather than collapsing into one generic "couldn't import this" error, because a silent or vague failure kills the app's only growth mechanism at the exact moment it was supposed to fire.

**Local-first and cloud-ready plan:** SwiftUI, fully local state for v1 — no accounts, no backend, on-device persistence with auto-save and forced-quit recovery that resumes exact in-progress geometry. Every reachable flow (play/edit/save/load/export/import) carries real empty/loading/success/error states, including corrupt-save recovery. The cloud-ready seam is a versioned level schema (a schema-version field from day one) plus a small persistence-layer protocol boundary (a `LevelStore`-style interface) so a future CloudKit-backed sync implementation is a new conformance, not a rewrite of the geometry engine or UI — retrofitting versioning after real share codes exist in the wild would be far worse than adding a version field now.

**Why this app deserves to exist:** it's the only entrant in this space treating "you can build what you can solve, and it reopens exactly as authored" as a testable engineering contract instead of a marketing claim — which is precisely the trust threshold a premium-puzzle buyer needs before a share code becomes something they actually send to a friend.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes cleanly. All six required outputs are present and concrete, each tied to specifics of Umbra rather than generic puzzle-game boilerplate:

- **Target user and use case**: names the premium-puzzle buyer with a concrete comparison set (Monument Valley, The Witness) and session length, plus the secondary "creator in the loop" segment — consistent with product_research and initial_discussion.
- **Core loop**: a real sequence (Vault → Play → Solve → Remix-into-Edit → Share/Save) rather than a vague phrase, correctly carrying forward the "remix into edit, not blank-canvas editor" structural decision locked in initial_discussion.
- **Paid value and subscription value**: passes the "functional not cosmetic" acceptance check explicitly — free tier is full editor/share (not gated-to-demo), paid tier is challenge packs and a pro editor subscription (symmetry tools, solve-history analytics, batch export). This is genuine content/capability, matching the contract's "never cosmetics-only" mandate.
- **Competitive wedge**: specific and falsifiable — round-trip artifact fidelity plus editor/play parity as an engineering contract, not a marketing claim, positioned against named genre patterns (Shadowmatic-style silhouette games).
- **Viral/niche growth angle**: correctly scoped as narrow, non-social, one-to-one sharing consistent with the locked non-goal on remote publishing, and it ties the three distinct import-failure states back to why growth-loop reliability matters commercially, not just technically.
- **Local-first and cloud-ready plan**: concrete seam (versioned schema field + `LevelStore`-style protocol boundary) that satisfies "cloud path without making cloud a dependency for v1" without overbuilding now.

The brief is internally consistent with every locked upstream decision (prompt_contract's hard requirements, product_research's audience/risk findings, initial_discussion's core loop and screen-level detail) and introduces no contradictions. Gemma3/Qwen's non-response this round is handled honestly (noted as CLI failure, not fabricated into false agreement or silently ignored). This is a usable, buildable artifact — the design phase can proceed directly from it without further ideation.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Looking at round 1, Codex and I independently produced full product briefs that agree on every required output, and Gemma3/Qwen both failed to return anything (CLI unavailable) — so there's nothing new to weigh against that alignment. I'll close this now with a synthesized brief.

**Paid value and subscription value:** The free starter vault contains five-plus authored, high-quality levels plus *full* editor and share functionality — a designer/share mode locked behind a paywall isn't a real designer/share mode, it's a demo, and a demo doesn't produce the friend-to-friend share codes this app depends on for growth. ## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 8 completely separate production-ready iOS games at the same time: exactly 2 unique idle games, 2 unique puzzle games, 2 drinking/party games playable by a group passing around ONE phone, and 2 large-group games where up to 10-12 players each connect from their own phone to one shared session (card-game formats welcome; prefer local peer-to-peer / GameKit / MultipeerConnectivity over a cloud backend so they stay local-first).

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Additional notes:
Monetization must sell REAL value (content, modes, mechanics, utility) — never cosmetics/skins alone.

Now generate at least 16 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 8, and build all 8 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Define the smallest valuable slice / immediate next steps that de-risk and validate the concept with minimal effort.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

I recommend we lock a tiny, high-confidence slice that proves the app’s identity before adding depth: a single canonical scene pipeline where one starter level can be solved, edited from that solved state, exported as a share artifact, then imported back to an identical playable level on the same device. This gives one emotionally complete loop and validates the only differentiator that matters for Umbra: Play/Edit/Share fidelity.

The scope for this slice is: one geometry engine pass that is deterministic and fully shared by play and edit; one versioned level schema with explicit encode/decode/diff; one vault with five minimal built-in levels stored in the same schema (quality can be rough initially); and an iOS flow of `Vault → Solve level 1 → Remix → Export → Corrupt test on import → Re-import valid code`. If that loop is stable, we can iterate levels and content fast without changing architecture.

The interactions this slice must have working are: place/move light source, place/rotate one or more rectangles, duplicate and delete an occluder, reset puzzle, live shadow overlap score rendered numerically and by outline cue (not just color), and edit gestures that are literally the same geometry objects/mutators used by play. Save state must auto-persist every interaction and recover exactly after relaunch, including in-progress edits and solved flags. Share must produce a compact artifact with version + checksum and import must distinguish: truncated/malformed, unsupported version, and structurally valid-but-unsolvable/invalid geometry.

The biggest objection to this direction is this: it can feel too small if I say “one level.” We can avoid that trap by keeping the full 5-level data model and vault scaffolding from day one, while only deeply validating and demonstrating through level 1 in the first pass. That avoids reopening the contract later while still keeping the slice buildable and emotionally satisfying.

Must-have interactions (no additions beyond this): drag/rotate object, duplicate/delete object, reset, solve status feedback with non-color cue, save/load local resume, export/import round-trip, and explicit error rendering for corrupt local save and bad import artifacts.

What to cut now: additional monetization UI (pack store/subscription UX), pro editor tools (symmetry solvers, constraint packs, analytics), advanced canvas cosmetics and transitions, haptic variants beyond one snap cue, onboarding onboarding with multiple screens, and any third-party backends.

Validation criteria to move forward:  
- The shared geometry engine produces identical `SolveResult` for repeated runs on the same seed and level.  
- One fixed level round-trip succeeds with zero structural drift (`encode -> decode -> diff == identical`) and exact re-render behavior.  
- At least three malformed import cases produce distinct recoverable messages and paths (retry, copy-fix hints, reset).  
- Local save corruption is detected and recovered deterministically (partial restore where possible, else clean reset with clear state).  
- A complete `Vault → Solve → Remix → Export → Import` run is possible with no crash, and the imported level starts in a valid solvable configuration.  
- Geometry edge tests include touching-only intersections, zero-area overlaps, near-collinear rotations, and light-inside-occluder cases; any failure blocks save with a specific reason.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

Here's my read on the smallest slice that actually proves Umbra, not just a placeholder version of it.

The temptation in a "smallest slice" phase is to cut down to something safe and boring — one level, static shadow, no share. I'd resist that hard. The entire commercial bet on this app, locked across five rounds of contract debate, is that "you can build what you can solve, and it reopens exactly as authored" is a real engineering claim, not a marketing line. If the first slice doesn't touch that claim, it hasn't actually de-risked anything — it's just proven you can draw shapes on a canvas, which nobody was worried about. So my position is: the smallest *valuable* slice is a single vertical spike through the entire loop — Play → Solve → Remix → Share → Import — on exactly one level, but with zero mocked layers underneath it. Real geometry engine, real solve-checker, real serialization format with the version field, real persistence. The thing being minimized is level *count* and UI *polish*, not architectural honesty.

Concretely: one hand-authored level, played on iPhone only for now, with the actual drag/rotate/light-source interaction, live shadow preview, and a match signal that's already non-color from day one (outline delta or numeric overlap, doesn't need haptic tuning yet but needs to exist, because retrofitting the core feedback loop later is exactly the kind of rework this phase should be preventing). A distinct solve confirmation moment — this is the emotional beat the phase rules are pointing at with "emotionally satisfying, not just technically minimal," and I'd treat it as non-negotiable even in the smallest slice, because a puzzle game that lets you win without ever feeling anything hasn't validated the concept, it's validated a hitbox. From there, a "remix this level" affordance into edit mode using the identical canvas and controls, then an export button that produces the real compact share code, and — this is the part I'd fight hardest to keep in scope — an actual import of that code back in, proven by a real encode→decode→diff check with zero drift, not a "trust me, it round-trips" comment. If that round trip doesn't work on day one with one level, it's not going to magically start working once there are five.

The must-have interactions, in priority order: drag and rotate on both the light source and at least one occluder shape; a live-updating shadow that recomputes on every gesture frame, not just on release, because "does this feel tactile" is exactly the question this slice exists to answer and a laggy or step-function shadow update would give a false negative; the non-color match signal; the solve-confirmation state; the remix-into-edit entry point; export-to-code and import-from-code, including at least one deliberately-triggered failure state (feed it a truncated code) so the error-handling architecture gets proven, not just assumed. Auto-save and forced-quit resume also belong in this slice even though they feel like "later polish" — they're cheap to build now on top of a single level's state and brutally expensive to retrofit once the persistence model has to support five levels plus in-progress edit state plus import history.

Cut list, stated plainly rather than silently dropped: four of the five starter levels (this slice ships with one, fully realized, not five mediocre ones); iPad-specific layout; any monetization UI, paywall, or IAP wiring; the pro editor tier (symmetry tools, solve-history analytics, batch export); full VoiceOver spatial-mental-model design — the phase gets baseline labeling on interactive elements, not the deeper accessibility strategy work the earlier phases correctly flagged as its own problem; onboarding beyond the bare minimum needed for someone unfamiliar with the app to get through the one level without being told what to do out of band; the two other import-failure states (version-mismatch, geometrically-invalid) beyond the one proven above — those get named as explicitly deferred, not forgotten, since the full three-state design is already locked as a hard requirement for the finished app.

Validation criteria I'd actually hold this to: the shadow-match has to feel good in under a minute of unguided play — if a fresh pair of hands can't tell what to do or doesn't feel a small thrill when it clicks into place, the mechanic itself needs rework before any more levels get built on top of it, and that's a far cheaper realization to have now than after five levels are authored on a mechanic that doesn't sing. The round-trip test has to pass with actual zero drift on this level plus at least one fuzzed permutation of it, not just the untouched original — a round trip that only works on the exact bytes you exported once isn't the claim we made. Force-quit during an in-progress edit has to resume to the exact same geometry, because if that breaks with one level's worth of state it will definitely break with more. And the one deliberately-malformed import has to produce a specific, real message, not a generic failure, since that's the whole point of proving the error-state architecture early rather than bolting it on later.

The single biggest objection to my own framing: building the full round-trip and remix machinery around just one level is more upfront engineering than a "true" minimal slice would ask for — a stricter minimalist would say ship Play alone first, prove the mechanic is fun, and only build Edit/Share once that's validated. I considered that and I'm rejecting it, because Play-alone doesn't touch the part of this app that's actually differentiated or actually risky. Any shadow-puzzle prototype can prove "dragging shapes to match a silhouette is satisfying" — that's table stakes, not the bet. The bet this whole project is built on is that editor/play parity and share-artifact fidelity hold up under real use, and that's precisely the thing five rounds of debate identified as the place a silent, undetected failure would do the most damage later. If I'm going to spend effort proving anything early, it should be the thing most likely to be secretly broken, not the thing everyone already assumes works.

**Gemma3 (local) (Delivery Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Okay, here's my first contribution to this phase:

Umbra is a local-first SwiftUI shadow-casting puzzle where a player positions a light source and reusable occluder primitives (move, rotate, duplicate, delete) to make an on-screen shadow match a target silhouette, with the same canvas and controls used in both play and design.

Primary user is a solo premium-puzzle player who wants high-signal, deterministic spatial thinking in short sessions, and a small creator cohort that enjoys making one good challenge and sending it to a friend as a personal challenge. The use case is a five-minute commute or break where someone wants a clear, tactile challenge, then optionally turns around and exports a level they made or edited.

The core loop is: learn one control, solve a level by precise spatial adjustment, receive immediate non-color overlap feedback, optionally edit that level (same controls and constraints as play), save/share locally with one explicit artifact format, and re-open it elsewhere with guaranteed same geometry and state.

Scope stays intentionally tight: local-only state with auto-save and forced-quit recovery, five-plus authored solvable levels with a real difficulty curve, strict Play/Edit parity, one canonical serialized level schema with version field, encode→decode→diff identity checks, explicit empty/loading/success/error paths in play/edit/save/load/export/import including corrupt local save and malformed/truncated artifact handling, and accessibility in the solve signal itself (outline, tick, haptic cue, and clear VoiceOver states). Non-goals: no backend, no accounts, no multiplayer, no feed/marketplace/remix ecosystem, no cosmetics-only monetization, no extra non-puzzle systems.

The measurable bar is mechanical: (1) each of the 5+ starter levels solvable from clean state; (2) import/export round-trip yields byte/geometry equality and no drift; (3) no regressions on zero-area, touching-only overlap, and near-edge rotation cases across fixed fixtures plus fuzzed geometry permutations; (4) the app resumes exact in-progress state after relaunch/force-quit; (5) any corrupt save or malformed/truncated/version-mismatched share artifact produces a specific, recoverable error message and a path forward — never a crash or a generic failure; (6) 1st-time user can discover all core controls (move, rotate, reset) within roughly 45 seconds via onboarding.

Biggest risk is not implementation speed but ambiguity: if the shadow match signal is ambiguous (especially for non-color accessibility), users cannot learn the system and will label the game as random; the final output must preserve the deterministic geometry contract and explicit import/export fidelity check as non-negotiable, because they are what make creator/share meaningful.
