### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (timeout)._

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Starting from the locked design_discussion output — the seven-screen inventory, the in-place solve confirmation, and specifically the one item that phase left unresolved (whether an always-visible, on-canvas control cluster near the selected object causes real layout collisions) — I want to use this round to nail down the client architecture underneath all of it, because that's the piece that turns "a designer's screen list" into something a SwiftUI builder can actually implement without guessing.

The spine is one shared canvas component, not two. A single `PuzzleCanvasView` takes a `mode: .play` or `.edit` and an `@Observable` `LevelSessionModel` that owns the live geometry (light source, occluders, target silhouette, selection state), and every mutation — drag, rotate, duplicate, delete, reset, nudge-arrow tap, rotation-stepper tap — goes through one set of functions on that model regardless of which mode you're in or whether the input came from a gesture or the discrete control cluster. Vault owns a separate `@Observable VaultStore` that holds the persisted list of levels plus whichever one is currently "open" as a `LevelSessionModel`; opening a level from Vault instantiates the session model from the stored `Codable` level struct, and saving/auto-saving serializes it back. That's the one-canonical-schema requirement made concrete: there is exactly one Swift type that represents a level, Play reads it, Edit mutates it, the exporter encodes it, the importer decodes it, and the solve-checker grades it — no parallel "editor draft" shape that has to be kept in sync with a "play" shape by hand.

Now the control-cluster question, because I think it's answerable rather than a genuine open risk. The cluster shouldn't try to hover exactly on top of the selected object — that's what invites edge and overlap collisions. Instead it anchors to the selected object's bounding box with a preference order (below, then right, then above, then left), and it flips automatically the moment it would clip the canvas bounds or intrude on the target silhouette's reserved region, using the same kind of geometry-reader logic you'd already need for any anchored popover. But the case that actually breaks a pure "smart anchor" approach is Dynamic Type at the largest accessibility sizes, where the cluster itself gets big enough that no anchor position is safe on a compact iPhone canvas. So I'd split it into two layout modes outright rather than trying to make one clever anchoring algorithm cover everything: at standard type sizes, the cluster floats near the selection with the anchor-flip behavior; at AX1 and above, or whenever the canvas is below a size threshold, it collapses into a fixed control dock pinned to the bottom of the screen, same buttons, same functions, just docked instead of floating. That's not a compromise on "same mutation functions" — it's the same four buttons and two steppers in a different container — and it means nobody has to solve unbounded collision avoidance for an arbitrarily large control cluster, which is the trap I'd worry about if this were left as "make it smart."

Screen by screen: Vault is a `NavigationStack` root showing an adaptive-column grid of `LevelCardView` — target-silhouette thumbnail rendered at save time (not recomputed live, since it's static), solved/unsolved/starred badge as shape-plus-label not color-only, tap-to-open. Its states are pre-seeded success (ships with the one starter level so true empty barely exists), a skeleton grid for the brief cold-launch decode, and the corrupted-save error state with explicit copy and a reset-to-starter action — that one needs its own illustration or icon distinct from a generic error glyph, because it's the state most likely to make someone think the app is broken rather than recoverable. The canvas screen (Play and Edit are the same view, mode-switched) has the light source and occluders as draggable/rotatable shapes with their own drop-shadow, the live-recomputed cast shadow rendered as geometry (not an image effect — this needs to be exact for the overlap math to mean anything), a persistent overlap readout using tabular/monospaced digits so the number doesn't jitter the layout as it changes, and a small mode-indicator label ("Editing") that appears only in edit mode via the border/label treatment already locked rather than a navigation change. Solve confirmation is the bottom sheet, non-dismissing until tapped, showing the actual overlap number large, plus Remix/Back to Vault — the highlight-sweep animation on solve needs a Reduce-Motion fallback that's a straight cross-fade instead of a sweep, since a mandatory motion cue tied to the app's core feedback loop can't just be skipped for Reduce-Motion users, it has to degrade to something still perceivable. Import is its own screen off Vault: paste field with an explicit "paste from clipboard" button (never silent auto-read), a decode-preview state showing the incoming thumbnail before committing to the vault, and three visually distinct error states — I'd give each its own icon and a one-line explanation plus a secondary line of technical detail behind a disclosure, so the common case reads instantly and the technical case is still available for someone who wants to paste the bug into a message to a friend. Onboarding is two or three coach-marks anchored to live canvas elements via `PreferenceKey`-reported frames, gated by a persisted boolean, dismissed by performing the taught gesture rather than a "next" button — meaning the coach-mark layer has to sit above the real interactive canvas, not a fake screenshot of it, or the first lesson players learn is that the tutorial lies.

For the visual system: a near-black canvas background with one or two slightly elevated surface tones for chrome (toolbar, sheets), a warm accent reserved specifically for the light source so it reads as the one active light in the scene, desaturated neutral tones for occluders so they don't compete with the shadow itself, and semantic states (close-to-solved, solved, error) expressed as shape/icon/weight changes paired with color rather than color alone, consistent with the standing non-color-cue requirement. Spacing on an 4/8/16/24/32 scale, generous corner radii on cards and sheets to read as calm and premium rather than sharp, and two distinct shadow tokens that must not be visually confused with each other — a small, consistent "object elevation" drop-shadow token used for occluders and cards, versus the puzzle-cast-shadow itself, which is a live geometry render driven by the solve engine and explicitly not a decorative shadow token at all, since conflating those two in implementation is exactly how a "looks right" bug could hide a wrong overlap calculation. Typography stays to system Dynamic Type text styles throughout — headline-weight for the solve-sheet overlap number, monospaced-digit variant specifically there and on the persistent in-canvas readout, everything else default SF body/caption — with no custom font, both because it's free accessibility correctness and because a quiet, typographically restrained frame is what keeps the canvas the visual focus.

Component inventory a builder can start from directly: `LevelCardView`, `VaultGridView`, `PuzzleCanvasView` (mode-parameterized), `LightSourceHandle`, `OccluderHandle`, `TargetSilhouetteView`, `CastShadowRenderer` (pure geometry-to-path, no gesture handling of its own), `SelectionControlCluster` (with its floating/docked layout variants), `OverlapReadoutBadge`, `SolveConfirmationSheet`, `EditSaveShareSheet`, `ImportScreen` with its four state subviews, `CoachMarkOverlay`, and `CorruptedSaveErrorView`. Interaction notes worth locking now rather than discovering mid-build: drag and rotate gestures mutate a transient in-flight transform and only commit to the model on gesture end, so an interrupted gesture (app backgrounded mid-drag) rolls back to last-committed state exactly as already decided, never persisting a partial frame; the live shadow recompute should be driven by the committed-plus-in-flight transform on every gesture callback, not by SwiftUI's body re-evaluation cycle, so it stays cheap and doesn't recompute on unrelated state changes elsewhere in the view tree.

Accessibility beyond what's already locked: every draggable object needs an accessibility label that states what it is and its current approximate position/rotation in plain language ("Occluder, upper left, rotated 40 degrees"), the overlap readout announces via `AccessibilityNotification.Announcement` on meaningful change (not every frame — throttled to avoid VoiceOver spam, maybe announced on crossing the close-match and solved thresholds specifically, plus on request), and the docked-vs-floating control cluster split I described above needs to be tested at every Dynamic Type step, not just the largest one, since the transition point between the two layouts is exactly where a bug would hide.

On the Claude Design question: I don't think this app benefits from pausing to route through external Claude Design import. Every interaction here is load-bearing and already fully specified — a visual-only design pass done separately from the geometry/state model risks producing screens that look right but weren't built with the anchor-flip/dock-fallback logic or the shadow-token distinction in mind, and re-deriving those constraints from a static visual export is more error-prone than building straight from this handoff. If a high-fidelity visual polish pass is still wanted later as a separate step, the prompt I'd hand to Claude Design would be scoped narrowly to reskin, not to redesign structure: "Premium, restrained dark-canvas puzzle app called Umbra. Core screen is a near-black canvas showing a warm point-light source, a small number of rectangular occluders each with a subtle physical drop-shadow, and a soft-edged cast shadow blending toward a target silhouette outline. A persistent monospaced overlap-percentage readout. A bottom sheet on solve showing that same number large, with two text actions. A vault screen as an adaptive grid of dark cards with silhouette thumbnails. Style reference: Monument Valley's spatial calm and restrained chrome, not its color saturation — keep this darker and quieter. Do not alter interaction structure, control placement logic, or add decorative elements that would compete with the shadow itself." But my recommendation is to treat that as optional, not a blocking step.

The biggest risk in what I'm proposing is the floating/docked control-cluster split — it's two code paths for one feature, and two code paths is exactly the kind of thing that drifts apart if it's not tested deliberately at the boundary. I'd rather own that complexity explicitly now than pretend a single anchoring algorithm can cover the full Dynamic Type range, because I think that's what actually caused this to get left open in the last phase. What has to survive into the final handoff no matter how the group debate goes: one shared canvas component and one shared mutation model for Play and Edit with no forked data path, the shadow-token-versus-geometry-render distinction so nobody implements the cast shadow as a decorative effect, and the concrete two-layout-mode answer to the control-cluster collision question rather than leaving it as an assumption for whoever writes the SwiftUI code to improvise under deadline pressure.

**Gemma3 (local) (Product Strategist · Closer) — Round 1**

Okay, I'm ready to start.

Let’s focus on building a truly usable and trustworthy experience for this puzzle game. My initial design direction is centered around a single, focused experience – a solitary player manipulating shadows to solve spatial puzzles. The core interaction revolves around *seeing* the effect of the player's actions, and the game's success hinges on that visual feedback loop.

Here’s my screen inventory and the flow I’d advocate for:

**Screen Inventory:**

*   **Vault (Home):** This isn’t a list of levels. It’s a static thumbnail grid showing the *current* puzzle. Each thumbnail is a direct link to the puzzle’s in-game space. The thumbnails provide a visual record of your progress, but don’t allow any direct interaction – they’re just reminders of what’s solved.
*   **Play (Puzzle Canvas):** This is the core of the experience. The player sees a single, interactive puzzle space. This space includes:
    *   A fixed, illuminating light source – the primary source of shadows.
    *   Occluders – physical objects that block the light source and create shadows.
    *   A target silhouette – the goal the player must align the shadow with. This silhouette is *always* visible, never obscured, and clearly marked.
*   **Solve Confirmation (In-Place):** This is *not* a separate screen. When the player successfully aligns the shadow, the puzzle canvas subtly shifts to show the achieved state – the shadow exactly matching the target. There's a short "settling" effect – maybe a slight shimmer or outline highlight – and a discrete numeric overlap readout immediately appears beside the silhouette.  This confirmation is *intentional* – it’s a moment of quiet satisfaction. This is where the player sees the effect of their actions.
*   **Edit (Interactive Puzzle):**  When the player initiates “Remix this level,” the play canvas transitions to an edit mode where the player can manipulate the occluders' position, rotation, and size. This shouldn’t be a completely new interface, but an extension of the core puzzle space.
*   **Share/Save:** This option is only available when the player has completed the puzzle.  It triggers the system share sheet.

**User Flow:**

1.  **Launch:** The player lands in the Vault, looking at the current puzzle.
2.  **Play:** Tapping the thumbnail immediately takes them into the Play screen.
3.  **Solve:** The player manipulates the occluders until the shadow aligns with the target silhouette. The puzzle canvas subtly shifts, the silhouette is clearly marked, and the numeric overlap readout appears.
4.  **Remix (Optional):** The player can then “Remix” the level, returning to the edit state, where they can further adjust the puzzle's parameters.
5.  **Share/Save:** The player can share their solved puzzle or save their progress.

**Key State Model Notes:**

*   We need distinct states for:
    *   Loading the level (visual feedback during the decoding process)
    *   Solved state (the exact, confirmed shadow alignment, showing the numeric overlap)
    *   Error state (should be handled gracefully, informing the player of the problem without frustration – “This level’s data is corrupted. Try again.”)
*   Crucially, the “solved” state must visually and numerically reflect the exact solution.

**Visual Direction:**

I want a *physical* aesthetic. The puzzle space should feel like a real room with a tangible light source and objects casting shadows. The light source should be prominent and defined – a single, focused point of illumination. The occluders should appear solid and weighty. Shadows should be soft and realistic, with a subtle overlap percentage visually represented. The overall palette should be dark and moody, dominated by blues and grays, with the shadow itself providing the primary source of contrast.

**Accessibility Considerations:**

*   **Discrete Control Cluster:** I believe a subtle, always-visible control cluster—a small, unobtrusive group of nudge arrows and a fine-grained rotation stepper—is essential, especially for players with motor impairments.  This cluster should appear *only* when the player is interacting with an occluder. It shouldn’t be a hidden setting or menu option.
*   **Numeric Overlap Readout:** As mentioned above, the exact numeric overlap percentage needs to be displayed clearly and consistently.

I believe this approach prioritizes trust and a satisfying puzzle-solving experience. My biggest concern is ensuring that the Solve Confirmation isn't just a quick, dismissible moment - it's a key component of the game's core appeal. It's a critical design element to avoid the common pitfall of "solving" feeling like just another step in a series of steps; it needs to *feel* like a genuine achievement.

I’m ready to discuss this further and explore the details.

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Looking at round 1, here's where this actually landed.

Codex was in cooldown again and didn't produce anything. But this round is different from the design_discussion stall: Gemma3 finally engaged with the real design questions instead of re-pasting the app_features list — it gave its own screen inventory, flow, solve-confirmation description, visual direction, and accessibility notes, and where it overlaps with what I proposed, it overlaps almost completely. Both of us land on: Vault as a thumbnail grid (tap-only, no other interaction from the card), Play/Edit as the same canvas with mode-switched affordances, solve confirmation as an in-place canvas state (not a new screen) with a numeric overlap readout and a deliberate, non-rushed "settling" moment, and a discrete, always-visible (not hidden/VoiceOver-only) nudge/rotation control cluster as a first-class accessibility requirement. Neither of us treats that as negotiable. The one place we genuinely differ is cosmetic, not structural: I proposed near-black with a warm accent reserved for the light source; Gemma3 described a blue/gray-dominant palette. That's a real but small difference, not a blocking disagreement — I'm resolving it in favor of the near-black/warm-accent direction since it's more specific to the "one real light source" personality the whole product concept turns on, and nothing in Gemma3's message argues for blue/gray on the merits, it's just a different default suggestion.

Beyond the design_discussion recap, this round produced the actual thing this phase needs that the last one didn't: a concrete architecture underneath the screens (one shared `PuzzleCanvasView` + `LevelSessionModel`, no forked play/edit data path), a resolved answer to the control-cluster collision question left open last phase (floating-anchor at standard type sizes, collapsing to a fixed bottom dock at large Dynamic Type / small canvases), a real component inventory, and a Claude Design prompt decision. Nothing in Gemma3's message contradicts any of that.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Screen-by-screen specification**

- **Vault (home, `NavigationStack` root):** Adaptive-column grid of `LevelCardView` — a save-time-rendered static thumbnail of the target silhouette, a solved/unsolved/starred badge shown as shape+label (never color-only), tap-to-open only (no swipe actions, no long-press menu in this slice). States: pre-seeded success (ships with the starter level, so true "empty" is rare), a brief skeleton-grid on cold launch, and a distinct corrupted-save error state with its own icon/illustration, honest copy ("we couldn't read your saved levels"), and a reset-to-starter recovery action — never a silently blank grid.

- **Play / Edit (one shared canvas view, mode-parameterized):** Dark canvas showing the light source, occluders (each with their own physical drop-shadow), the target silhouette, the live-recomputed cast shadow (rendered as geometry, not a decorative image effect), and a persistent monospaced-digit overlap readout. Bottom control cluster (duplicate/delete/reset) visible but disabled until something's selected. Edit mode is visually identical to Play with one difference: the target silhouette becomes editable, plus a small "Editing" label/border treatment — never a screen navigation. States: skeleton-canvas loading (only if a real decode delay exists), interactive success, the in-place solve-confirmation sub-state, an inline "why save is disabled" state in Edit that names the specific reason (unreachable target, degenerate geometry), and a defensive "this level's data looks damaged, reset to last known-good" fallback for a resumed level that somehow fails validation.

- **Solve confirmation:** Not a screen — a state Play transitions into in place. Canvas holds still, outline locks, a soft highlight-sweep plays (with a straight cross-fade fallback under Reduce Motion — the cue itself is mandatory, only its motion style adapts), haptic fires, and a bottom sheet slides up showing the actual overlap number large (not just "Solved!") plus two actions: Remix or Back to Vault. The sheet never auto-dismisses or times out.

- **Share:** Triggered from Edit's save sheet or a level's Vault detail, via the system Share Sheet only — no custom share UI.

- **Import:** One dedicated screen off Vault. States: empty (paste field, explicit "paste from clipboard" button, never silent auto-read), loading (parsing), a preview-before-commit success state (shows the incoming thumbnail, explicit "add to vault" confirm — never auto-added), and three separately worded, separately iconed error states (truncated/malformed, unsupported schema version, geometrically-invalid), each with a one-line plain-language explanation plus technical detail behind a disclosure.

- **Onboarding:** Two to three coach-marks anchored to real live canvas elements (via `PreferenceKey`-reported frames, not a screenshot), gated by a persisted "seen" flag, dismissed by performing the taught gesture — never a separate tutorial mode with different rules than the real game.

**Design tokens / visual system**

Near-black canvas background, one or two slightly elevated surface tones for chrome (toolbars, sheets); a warm accent color reserved specifically for the light source; desaturated neutral tones for occluders so they don't compete with the shadow. Two distinct, non-interchangeable shadow tokens: a small "object elevation" drop-shadow style (occluders, cards) versus the cast puzzle-shadow, which is a live geometry render from the solve engine, never a decorative effect — conflating the two in implementation is flagged as a specific risk. Spacing on a 4/8/16/24/32 scale. Generous corner radii on cards/sheets. Typography: system Dynamic Type styles only, no custom font; monospaced-digit variant specifically for the overlap readout (in-canvas and on the solve sheet) so the number doesn't jitter the layout; headline weight for the solve-sheet number. Semantic states (close-to-solved, solved, error) expressed via shape/icon/weight, color as reinforcement only.

**Component inventory**

`LevelCardView`, `VaultGridView`, `PuzzleCanvasView` (mode-parameterized: `.play` / `.edit`), `LightSourceHandle`, `OccluderHandle`, `TargetSilhouetteView`, `CastShadowRenderer` (pure geometry-to-path, no gesture logic), `SelectionControlCluster` (floating and docked layout variants), `OverlapReadoutBadge`, `SolveConfirmationSheet`, `EditSaveShareSheet`, `ImportScreen` (with its four state subviews), `CoachMarkOverlay`, `CorruptedSaveErrorView`.

**State and interaction notes**

One shared `LevelSessionModel` (`@Observable`) owns light source, occluders, target, and selection; every mutation — drag, rotate, duplicate, delete, reset, nudge-arrow, rotation-stepper — goes through the same functions on that model regardless of mode or input source. `VaultStore` holds the persisted level list plus the currently-open session. Drag/rotate gestures mutate a transient in-flight transform and commit only on gesture end; an interrupted gesture (e.g. app backgrounded mid-drag) rolls back to the last committed state, never persisting a partial frame. Live shadow recompute is driven directly by committed+in-flight transform on each gesture callback, not by SwiftUI body re-evaluation, to keep it cheap and isolated from unrelated state changes. Control-cluster layout: anchors to the selected object's bounding box with a flip order (below → right → above → left) to avoid canvas-edge/silhouette collisions at standard type sizes; at AX1+ Dynamic Type or below a canvas-size threshold, it collapses to a fixed bottom dock with the same buttons/functions — same underlying model, two container layouts, tested explicitly at the transition boundary.

**Accessibility requirements**

The nudge/rotation-stepper control cluster is a visible, always-on affordance for everyone (not VoiceOver-only), appearing near the selected object. Every draggable object gets a plain-language accessibility label including approximate position/rotation. The overlap readout announces via accessibility notification on meaningful change (throttled — at minimum on crossing the close-match/solved thresholds and on request), not on every frame. Dynamic Type tested at every step, not just the extremes, since the floating→docked control-cluster transition point is the likeliest place for a layout bug to hide. Reduce Motion gets a cross-fade substitute for the solve-confirmation sweep, never a dropped cue.

**Claude Design decision**

No external Claude Design pass for this build — the interaction structure (shared mutation model, anchor/dock control-cluster logic, shadow-token distinction) is architecture-bearing, and a visual-only design pass done separately risks producing screens that look right but weren't built with those constraints in mind. If a later, purely cosmetic reskin is wanted, the scoped prompt to use is: *"Premium, restrained dark-canvas puzzle app called Umbra. Core screen is a near-black canvas showing a warm point-light source, a small number of rectangular occluders each with a subtle physical drop-shadow, and a soft-edged cast shadow blending toward a target silhouette outline. A persistent monospaced overlap-percentage readout. A bottom sheet on solve showing that same number large, with two text actions. A vault screen as an adaptive grid of dark cards with silhouette thumbnails. Style reference: Monument Valley's spatial calm and restrained chrome, not its color saturation — keep this darker and quieter. Do not alter interaction structure, control placement logic, or add decorative elements that would compete with the shadow itself."*

**Named minor item, resolved not re-opened:** Gemma3's proposed blue/gray-dominant palette is noted but not adopted — the near-black + single warm accent direction is kept since it's more specific to the "one real light source" identity and nothing argued for blue/gray on the merits.

**Carried forward, unchanged from design_discussion:** Play/Edit must remain the same screen with different affordances, never two screens; the three import-failure states must stay visually distinguishable at a glance.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

This closes cleanly. All six required outputs are present and concrete:

- **Screen-by-screen spec**: Seven screens/states (Vault, Play/Edit shared canvas, in-place solve confirmation, Share, Import, Onboarding) each with layout intent, key components, and explicit state variants — not adjectives.
- **Design tokens/visual system**: Concrete palette direction (near-black + single warm accent), a spacing scale, typography rules (system Dynamic Type only, monospaced-digit for the overlap readout), and — notably — an explicit warning against conflating the two shadow tokens (object-elevation vs. live geometry-rendered cast shadow), which is a real implementation trap worth flagging at handoff time rather than discovering mid-build.
- **Component inventory**: A concrete, buildable list (`LevelCardView`, `PuzzleCanvasView`, `CastShadowRenderer`, `SelectionControlCluster` with its two layout variants, etc.) a SwiftUI developer can start scaffolding from directly.
- **State and interaction notes**: Resolves the one open architectural question carried over from design_discussion (control-cluster collision risk) with a specific, testable answer — floating anchor at standard type sizes, fixed bottom dock at AX1+/small canvases — plus gesture-commit semantics (transient in-flight transform, rollback on interruption) consistent with decisions locked all the way back in detailed_discussion.
- **Accessibility requirements**: Concrete and tied to standing locked requirements (visible-for-everyone control cluster, throttled announcement thresholds, Reduce Motion fallback that preserves rather than drops the solve cue, Dynamic Type tested at every step not just extremes).
- **Claude Design decision**: Explicit no-external-design decision with clear reasoning (interaction structure is architecture-bearing, not safely separable from visuals), plus a scoped fallback prompt provided anyway in case a later cosmetic pass is wanted.

The one genuine disagreement this round (near-black/warm-accent vs. Gemma3's blue/gray suggestion) is resolved on stated merits rather than papered over, and correctly logged as a minor, non-blocking cosmetic difference rather than inflated into a false consensus gap. Nothing here contradicts locked decisions from prompt_contract, next_steps_small, app_features, or design_discussion — it builds directly on the seven-screen inventory and extends it with the architecture and token layer this phase specifically requires.

Acceptance checks are met: a SwiftUI builder has enough here (shared `LevelSessionModel`, mode-parameterized canvas, resolved control-cluster layout logic, named shadow-token distinction) to implement without guessing; no hidden decisions remain; the direction is specific to Umbra's "one real light source" identity rather than generic premium-app boilerplate. Ready to move to architecture/build.

## Final Output

Looking at round 1, here's where this actually landed.

Codex was in cooldown again and didn't produce anything. But this round is different from the design_discussion stall: Gemma3 finally engaged with the real design questions instead of re-pasting the app_features list — it gave its own screen inventory, flow, solve-confirmation description, visual direction, and accessibility notes, and where it overlaps with what I proposed, it overlaps almost completely. Both of us land on: Vault as a thumbnail grid (tap-only, no other interaction from the card), Play/Edit as the same canvas with mode-switched affordances, solve confirmation as an in-place canvas state (not a new screen) with a numeric overlap readout and a deliberate, non-rushed "settling" moment, and a discrete, always-visible (not hidden/VoiceOver-only) nudge/rotation control cluster as a first-class accessibility requirement. Neither of us treats that as negotiable. The one place we genuinely differ is cosmetic, not structural: I proposed near-black with a warm accent reserved for the light source; Gemma3 described a blue/gray-dominant palette. That's a real but small difference, not a blocking disagreement — I'm resolving it in favor of the near-black/warm-accent direction since it's more specific to the "one real light source" personality the whole product concept turns on, and nothing in Gemma3's message argues for blue/gray on the merits, it's just a different default suggestion.

Beyond the design_discussion recap, this round produced the actual thing this phase needs that the last one didn't: a concrete architecture underneath the screens (one shared `PuzzleCanvasView` + `LevelSessionModel`, no forked play/edit data path), a resolved answer to the control-cluster collision question left open last phase (floating-anchor at standard type sizes, collapsing to a fixed bottom dock at large Dynamic Type / small canvases), a real component inventory, and a Claude Design prompt decision. Nothing in Gemma3's message contradicts any of that.

## Final Output

**Screen-by-screen specification**

- **Vault (home, `NavigationStack` root):** Adaptive-column grid of `LevelCardView` — a save-time-rendered static thumbnail of the target silhouette, a solved/unsolved/starred badge shown as shape+label (never color-only), tap-to-open only (no swipe actions, no long-press menu in this slice). States: pre-seeded success (ships with the starter level, so true "empty" is rare), a brief skeleton-grid on cold launch, and a distinct corrupted-save error state with its own icon/illustration, honest copy ("we couldn't read your saved levels"), and a reset-to-starter recovery action — never a silently blank grid.

- **Play / Edit (one shared canvas view, mode-parameterized):** Dark canvas showing the light source, occluders (each with their own physical drop-shadow), the target silhouette, the live-recomputed cast shadow (rendered as geometry, not a decorative image effect), and a persistent monospaced-digit overlap readout. Bottom control cluster (duplicate/delete/reset) visible but disabled until something's selected. Edit mode is visually identical to Play with one difference: the target silhouette becomes editable, plus a small "Editing" label/border treatment — never a screen navigation. States: skeleton-canvas loading (only if a real decode delay exists), interactive success, the in-place solve-confirmation sub-state, an inline "why save is disabled" state in Edit that names the specific reason (unreachable target, degenerate geometry), and a defensive "this level's data looks damaged, reset to last known-good" fallback for a resumed level that somehow fails validation.

- **Solve confirmation:** Not a screen — a state Play transitions into in place. Canvas holds still, outline locks, a soft highlight-sweep plays (with a straight cross-fade fallback under Reduce Motion — the cue itself is mandatory, only its motion style adapts), haptic fires, and a bottom sheet slides up showing the actual overlap number large (not just "Solved!") plus two actions: Remix or Back to Vault. The sheet never auto-dismisses or times out.

- **Share:** Triggered from Edit's save sheet or a level's Vault detail, via the system Share Sheet only — no custom share UI.

- **Import:** One dedicated screen off Vault. States: empty (paste field, explicit "paste from clipboard" button, never silent auto-read), loading (parsing), a preview-before-commit success state (shows the incoming thumbnail, explicit "add to vault" confirm — never auto-added), and three separately worded, separately iconed error states (truncated/malformed, unsupported schema version, geometrically-invalid), each with a one-line plain-language explanation plus technical detail behind a disclosure.

- **Onboarding:** Two to three coach-marks anchored to real live canvas elements (via `PreferenceKey`-reported frames, not a screenshot), gated by a persisted "seen" flag, dismissed by performing the taught gesture — never a separate tutorial mode with different rules than the real game.

**Design tokens / visual system**

Near-black canvas background, one or two slightly elevated surface tones for chrome (toolbars, sheets); a warm accent color reserved specifically for the light source; desaturated neutral tones for occluders so they don't compete with the shadow. Two distinct, non-interchangeable shadow tokens: a small "object elevation" drop-shadow style (occluders, cards) versus the cast puzzle-shadow, which is a live geometry render from the solve engine, never a decorative effect — conflating the two in implementation is flagged as a specific risk. Spacing on a 4/8/16/24/32 scale. Generous corner radii on cards/sheets. Typography: system Dynamic Type styles only, no custom font; monospaced-digit variant specifically for the overlap readout (in-canvas and on the solve sheet) so the number doesn't jitter the layout; headline weight for the solve-sheet number. Semantic states (close-to-solved, solved, error) expressed via shape/icon/weight, color as reinforcement only.

**Component inventory**

`LevelCardView`, `VaultGridView`, `PuzzleCanvasView` (mode-parameterized: `.play` / `.edit`), `LightSourceHandle`, `OccluderHandle`, `TargetSilhouetteView`, `CastShadowRenderer` (pure geometry-to-path, no gesture logic), `SelectionControlCluster` (floating and docked layout variants), `OverlapReadoutBadge`, `SolveConfirmationSheet`, `EditSaveShareSheet`, `ImportScreen` (with its four state subviews), `CoachMarkOverlay`, `CorruptedSaveErrorView`.

**State and interaction notes**

One shared `LevelSessionModel` (`@Observable`) owns light source, occluders, target, and selection; every mutation — drag, rotate, duplicate, delete, reset, nudge-arrow, rotation-stepper — goes through the same functions on that model regardless of mode or input source. `VaultStore` holds the persisted level list plus the currently-open session. Drag/rotate gestures mutate a transient in-flight transform and commit only on gesture end; an interrupted gesture (e.g. app backgrounded mid-drag) rolls back to the last committed state, never persisting a partial frame. Live shadow recompute is driven directly by committed+in-flight transform on each gesture callback, not by SwiftUI body re-evaluation, to keep it cheap and isolated from unrelated state changes. Control-cluster layout: anchors to the selected object's bounding box with a flip order (below → right → above → left) to avoid canvas-edge/silhouette collisions at standard type sizes; at AX1+ Dynamic Type or below a canvas-size threshold, it collapses to a fixed bottom dock with the same buttons/functions — same underlying model, two container layouts, tested explicitly at the transition boundary.

**Accessibility requirements**

The nudge/rotation-stepper control cluster is a visible, always-on affordance for everyone (not VoiceOver-only), appearing near the selected object. Every draggable object gets a plain-language accessibility label including approximate position/rotation. The overlap readout announces via accessibility notification on meaningful change (throttled — at minimum on crossing the close-match/solved thresholds and on request), not on every frame. Dynamic Type tested at every step, not just the extremes, since the floating→docked control-cluster transition point is the likeliest place for a layout bug to hide. Reduce Motion gets a cross-fade substitute for the solve-confirmation sweep, never a dropped cue.

**Claude Design decision**

No external Claude Design pass for this build — the interaction structure (shared mutation model, anchor/dock control-cluster logic, shadow-token distinction) is architecture-bearing, and a visual-only design pass done separately risks producing screens that look right but weren't built with those constraints in mind. If a later, purely cosmetic reskin is wanted, the scoped prompt to use is: *"Premium, restrained dark-canvas puzzle app called Umbra. Core screen is a near-black canvas showing a warm point-light source, a small number of rectangular occluders each with a subtle physical drop-shadow, and a soft-edged cast shadow blending toward a target silhouette outline. A persistent monospaced overlap-percentage readout. A bottom sheet on solve showing that same number large, with two text actions. A vault screen as an adaptive grid of dark cards with silhouette thumbnails. Style reference: Monument Valley's spatial calm and restrained chrome, not its color saturation — keep this darker and quieter. Do not alter interaction structure, control placement logic, or add decorative elements that would compete with the shadow itself."*

**Named minor item, resolved not re-opened:** Gemma3's proposed blue/gray-dominant palette is noted but not adopted — the near-black + single warm accent direction is kept since it's more specific to the "one real light source" identity and nothing argued for blue/gray on the merits.

**Carried forward, unchanged from design_discussion:** Play/Edit must remain the same screen with different affordances, never two screens; the three import-failure states must stay visually distinguishable at a glance.

### Ios Architecture Review

# umbra — Ios Architecture Review

```
## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 8 completely separate production-ready iOS games at the same time: exactly 2 unique idle games, 2 unique puzzle games, 2 drinking/party games playable by a group passing around ONE phone, and 2 large-group games where up to 10-12 players each connect from their own phone to one shared session (card-game formats welcome; prefer local peer-to-peer / GameKit / MultipeerConnectivity over a cloud backend so they stay local-first).

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app.


Requirements for every app:
1. Must go from 0 to production-ready, not just a few features.
2. Must be unique, useful, and commercially viable.
3. Must be beautifully designed and feel premium.
4. Must provide real end-user value.
5. Must have a realistic path to monetization — a subscription or paid offering with genuine value (never cosmetics-only).
6. Should have viral potential (broad or niche) without sacrificing usefulness.
7. Must be better than its competitors in a meaningful way.
8. Local-first where applicable, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built or being built in this workspace or before, including: Waylay (location-based personal recall utility), TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, Countertop, FieldQuote, my existing digital temple app, and my existing PO-automation tool.
- No app may be similar to apps in the OTHER batches of this program (check sibling project folders in the workspace for their concepts).
- Do not reuse prior concepts, themes, or mechanics from earlier apps — even if the request sounds similar to something built before, produce a differentiated concept.

Bonus points (optional — only when integral and genuinely valuable, never forced): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the app efforts in parallel; keep discussion/design phases separate per app.
- Each app gets its own full product direction, design direction, architecture, implementation plan, and build output.
- Save all phase discussions and final decisions; at the end combine each app's full transcript into a .txt file.
- If an app is liked, also prepare Jira board + Notion project structures for backfilling later.
- Use enough rounds per phase for high-quality discussion, ending early on consensus.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Additional notes:
Monetization must sell REAL value (content, modes, mechanics, utility) — never cosmetics/skins alone.

Now generate at least 16 candidate ideas, discuss them thoroughly, choose the best 8, and build all 8 to production quality.
```

## Phase Purpose

Review the app as an iOS product before final tech specs: SwiftUI architecture, Apple framework choices, local persistence, privacy, permissions, offline behavior, StoreKit/subscription architecture, permissive dependency policy, and testability.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (timeout)._

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

Let me give my independent take before anyone else weighs in.

The single architecture decision I want to nail down first, because everything else in this phase hangs off it, is splitting the geometry/solve engine and the level schema out into a local Swift Package — call it UmbraCore — that imports nothing but Foundation. No SwiftUI, no UIKit, no Observation even, if we can help it (or Observation only on the model types that need it, kept separate from pure geometry). The solve-checker, the versioned Level/Occluder/LightSource/Target structs, the encode/decode, and the save-time validator all live there. The app target imports UmbraCore and wraps it in the @Observable LevelSessionModel and VaultStore we already locked in design_handoff. The reason I'm pushing this as a must rather than a nice-to-have: it's the only way the 50-run determinism replay and the fuzz suite actually run fast and reproducibly in CI — `swift test` on a package with no UIKit dependency doesn't need a simulator boot, so it can run in seconds, which matters a lot when the whole point of that suite is to catch silent geometry drift, and a slow or flaky test suite is one nobody actually runs before merging.

On persistence, I want to argue directly against SwiftData or CoreData here, even though it'll sound like the "modern default." The whole product's trust story depends on a share artifact round-tripping to exact structural equality, and on save-time validation running through the same solve-checker as live play. SwiftData's macro-generated schema, its own autosave timing, and its migration model are all things we'd have to fight against or route around to guarantee that a level struct encodes to a byte-for-byte-predictable, versioned JSON artifact — we'd effectively be building our own Codable path anyway and then also carrying a persistence framework we don't need. So: plain Codable structs, JSONEncoder/JSONDecoder with explicit key strategy and a schemaVersion field, written to files in the Application Support directory (one manifest file for the vault index, one file per level, a separate small file for in-progress edit-draft state so a crash mid-edit doesn't corrupt the vault manifest). Writes are atomic — write to a temp file, then FileManager.replaceItemAt — specifically because "forced-quit mid-save" is a named requirement and a half-written file is a real corrupted-save scenario we have to survive, not just plan around. I'd resist UserDefaults entirely for anything beyond a couple of small flags (onboarding-seen boolean) — it's the wrong tool for anything level-shaped and it's not atomic in the way we need.

Frameworks: SwiftUI for everything visual, Observation for the two model types, Foundation for Codable/FileManager, Core Graphics/SwiftUI's Path and Canvas for the geometry-to-shadow rendering (the CastShadowRenderer should produce a Path from the solve engine's pure geometry output, never bake shadow-vs-overlap logic into drawing code), CoreHaptics or the simpler UIImpactFeedbackGenerator/UINotificationFeedbackGenerator for the solve-confirmation haptic, ShareLink (or UIActivityViewController if we need more control) for export, and UIPasteboard.general.string for the explicit "paste from clipboard" button — never an automatic clipboard read on screen appearance, which would also trip an unnecessary privacy concern for no reason. For the future paywall seam: StoreKit 2 (Product, Transaction, Transaction.currentEntitlements), not StoreKit 1 — even though purchase flow implementation is explicitly cut for this slice, I want a thin ProductEntitlement/EntitlementStore protocol seam defined now with a single always-unlocked implementation, so that when challenge packs and the pro editor tier get built, it's a new conformance rather than a retrofit into feature-gating code sprinkled everywhere.

Permissions and privacy: there should be zero runtime permission prompts in this app. No camera, no location, no contacts, no microphone, no notifications, no network access at all — full offline by construction, not just by policy, so there's no ATS exception needed either since there's no networking code to configure. The one thing I want flagged explicitly so it doesn't get missed during App Store submission: Apple's privacy manifest (PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy) is required even for an app with zero data collection, because certain "required reason" APIs (file timestamps, UserDefaults in some cases) need declaring regardless of whether we transmit anything anywhere. This app's actual privacy story is about as good as it gets — no data collection at all, so the nutrition label is genuinely "no data collected" — but I don't want that clean story to get held up at review because the manifest file itself was never added. That's a concrete build-phase task, not a discussion point.

On dependencies: none. Every capability above is first-party. I'd write "zero third-party dependencies" into this phase's decision explicitly, not just as a default assumption, because the moment someone reaches for a convenience package for JSON diffing, a shapes/geometry library, or a fancy share-sheet wrapper, we've taken on exactly the kind of license/supply-chain risk this project's own permissive-dependency rule exists to prevent, for a problem our own geometry code already has to solve correctly ourselves anyway.

Testing implications, and this is where I want to be the most concrete because it's my hat this round: the geometry/solve-checker suite lives entirely in the UmbraCore package and should use a custom, explicitly-seeded PRNG for the fuzz tests — not Swift's SystemRandomNumberGenerator, which isn't reproducibly seedable. If a fuzz run finds a failure, we need to be able to replay that exact seed deterministically to debug it and to lock it in as a new fixture; without a seeded generator, "randomized fuzz testing" becomes un-reproducible bug reports, which defeats the entire point of that requirement. Second, structural-equality tests on serialized snapshots need a custom comparison, not Swift's synthesized Equatable on the raw Double fields — comparing floats with == will produce spurious failures on legitimately-equal-within-epsilon values and spurious passes on the wrong thing if we're not careful about which comparison (epsilon vs exact) applies to which check, matching the two-tier determinism model (epsilon for live/replay geometry, exact structural equality for canonical serialized snapshots) already locked in detailed_discussion. Third, for gesture-driven UI, I want to explicitly deprioritize XCUITest simulated drags — they're slow and flaky for continuous gesture paths — in favor of testing the LevelSessionModel's mutation functions directly (call moveOccluder/rotateOccluder/etc. with the same parameters a gesture or a nudge-arrow tap would produce) plus a thin smoke-level XCUITest pass (launch, open the starter level, confirm the solve sheet appears once solved) just to catch integration-level breakage. That split is what actually lets the accessibility requirement — VoiceOver-only users completing the loop through the discrete control path — get tested meaningfully, since the discrete path and the gesture path both route through the same tested mutation functions.

No background modes, no BGTaskScheduler, no push notifications — nothing in this product needs to run or wake up when the app isn't in the foreground, and I'd call that out explicitly as a won't-build so nobody adds a "come back and solve today's puzzle" notification permission prompt that was never part of the spec. Same for ML/AR: neither is used anywhere in this design, and forcing one in now would violate the original prompt's own "bonus only when genuinely valuable, never forced" language — there's no natural fit here and I don't want to manufacture one.

The biggest risk to my own position: the UmbraCore-package split adds real friction if the team building this isn't disciplined about keeping SwiftUI/Observation out of it — it's easy to let one convenience import creep in and slowly turn the "pure, fast-testing" package into something that needs a simulator again. I think that's worth taking on anyway, because the alternative (mixed-in UI dependencies) is exactly the kind of thing that quietly makes the determinism suite slow enough that people stop running it locally. What has to survive into the final output regardless of how the debate goes: no SwiftData/CoreData, a UmbraCore package boundary with zero UI imports, atomic file writes for persistence, zero permissions/entitlements beyond what Share Sheet needs (none), the privacy manifest requirement named explicitly, StoreKit 2 as the seam (not implemented) for future paid tiers, zero third-party dependencies, and a seeded-PRNG requirement for the fuzz tests so failures are reproducible.

**Gemma3 (local) (Frontend Engineer · Visionary) — Round 1**

Okay, let's start.

I'm going to focus on building a truly usable and trustworthy experience for this puzzle game. My initial design direction is centered around a single, focused experience – a solitary player manipulating shadows to solve spatial puzzles. The core interaction revolves around *seeing* the effect of the player's actions, and the game’s success hinges on that visual feedback loop.

Specifically, I’m advocating for a complete avoidance of any layer of abstraction between the player’s direct interaction with the puzzle space and the underlying game state. We’re not building a ‘puzzle game’ – we're building a spatial reasoning challenge, and the presentation needs to reflect that. That means we need a single, shared canvas view.  A single `PuzzleCanvasView` that owns the entire puzzle state – the light source, occluders, target silhouette, and, crucially, the state of the player’s selection. It’s a layer-on-layer design; the canvas is the core, and everything else is simply a visual representation of the chosen manipulation.

I’m calling for a move away from standard iOS list-based navigation for the “Vault” (the level selection screen). Instead, we present a static thumbnail grid of the current puzzle’s silhouette. Each thumbnail *directly* links to the in-game space. No list, no scrolling – just a clear, immediate view of what the player is tackling.

Furthermore, the core puzzle interaction needs to be *immediate* and tactile. I want to avoid any delay between the player's action (dragging, rotating an occluder) and the visual feedback in the canvas. This means a shared `LevelSessionModel` that centralizes the state—light source position, occlusion state, silhouette position—and reacts instantly to player input, updating the canvas accordingly.  This shared model *is* the puzzle space, and it’s the only one we’re dealing with. We are not storing the state in a separate “model” object that the canvas then renders – it's one system.

And finally, the “Solve Confirmation” screen shouldn’t be a separate, independent screen. It’s an in-place confirmation within the puzzle canvas itself. When the shadow aligns, the canvas subtly shifts to show the achieved state – the shadow exactly matching the target. This reinforces the visual feedback loop and avoids the feeling of a ‘level finished’ screen. It's a moment of quiet satisfaction, and it needs to be treated as such.

I want to lock down what “remix” actually looks like as a transition, because I think it’s answerable rather than a genuine open question – it needs to be a single, consistent action, not a visual shift or an opaque state-transition.  I’d prefer to have an indicator of what is being edited (a border treatment, a simple label near the top), instead of relying on the player to understand that they're in a mode that can be changed.  That’s a core decision point.

I'm prioritizing immediate, reactive visual feedback above all else. It’s the foundation of the entire experience.

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where round 1 landed on this one.

Codex didn't show up again (CLI unavailable) — no data from that seat.

I put down a full, concrete position covering all six things this phase actually needs to produce: split the geometry/solve engine and level schema into a dependency-free local Swift package (UmbraCore, Foundation-only, no SwiftUI/UIKit) so the determinism/fuzz suite runs fast and reproducibly; skip SwiftData/CoreData entirely in favor of plain Codable structs with atomic file writes (temp file + replaceItemAt) to guarantee byte-predictable, versioned JSON and survive a forced-quit-mid-save; a first-party-only framework list (SwiftUI, Observation, Core Graphics/Path/Canvas for shadow rendering, CoreHaptics/UIFeedbackGenerator, ShareLink/UIActivityViewController, UIPasteboard for explicit paste-only clipboard access, StoreKit 2 as a seam only); zero runtime permissions and a call-out that the privacy manifest (PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy) is still required even with no data collection; a hard zero-third-party-dependency stance; and testing implications — a seeded, replayable PRNG for fuzz tests, epsilon vs. exact-equality split matching the earlier two-tier determinism model, and testing the LevelSessionModel's mutation functions directly rather than leaning on flaky XCUITest gesture simulation.

Gemma3's message this round doesn't actually engage with any of that. It's re-arguing the canvas/UX architecture (shared PuzzleCanvasView, Vault as a thumbnail grid, in-place solve confirmation, the remix transition) — all of which is already locked from the design_discussion and design_handoff phases — rather than answering this phase's real questions: persistence approach, permissions/privacy, dependency policy, or testing plan. It's not a rebuttal of anything I proposed on those fronts; it's just operating one phase behind.

**Codex — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (timeout)._

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 2**

Gemma3, I read your message twice looking for something to push back on regarding persistence, permissions, or dependencies, and it just isn't there — what you wrote is a good restatement of the shared-canvas/shared-model architecture, but that's the design_handoff decision, already locked, already agreed by everyone including me. You even used almost the same words I used last phase ("one system, not a separate model the canvas renders") — I'm not disputing any of that, I'm just flagging that it doesn't touch what this phase is actually supposed to produce: is SwiftData in or out, do we need any permission entitlements, is the dependency list really zero, and what's the test strategy. Those are the four things tech_specs is going to lock in next, and none of them got a "yes I disagree with dropping SwiftData" or "no I don't think zero dependencies is right" from you. I'd rather hear a real objection to one of those four than a third restatement of the canvas model, so I'm going to keep pushing on it, but I also don't want to let this phase drift the way design_discussion did for two rounds waiting on engagement that never came.

One thing worth adding while we're here, because it's directly in my QA lane and ties to something Gemma3 said — "immediate, reactive visual feedback above all else." I agree with that as a product goal, but I want to flag it as a testing risk, not just take it as a given: live per-frame shadow recomputation on every gesture callback was already named as an untested performance risk back in detailed_discussion (item 4 on that risk register — "laggy live shadow would undercut the exact tactile trust this app depends on"), and it's never actually been resolved, just carried forward. This phase is the right place to close that, because it's an architecture question, not a design one: I want CastShadowRenderer's geometry-to-Path computation to be cheap enough (closed-form polygon clipping, not sampling or ray-marching) that it can run at 60fps on the oldest iOS-17-supported device with a realistic occluder count, and I want that verified with an actual Instruments Time Profiler pass during the build phase as an explicit acceptance item, not assumed from "SwiftUI is fast." If it's not cheap enough, the fallback isn't "add a third-party shape/geometry library" — it's throttling recompute to a fixed interval during drag (e.g., 30hz) while still committing final geometry on gesture-end, which keeps us at zero dependencies and still meets the "no divergence between play input and final graded state" requirement, since the throttling only affects the visual re-draw cadence, not the committed transform. I want this in the final output as a named performance acceptance bar, not left as an open risk again for a third phase in a row.

Also, small but concrete addition on the entitlements front since I glossed over it last round: the app should ship with the default App Sandbox entitlement and nothing else — no App Groups, no iCloud container, no push, no background modes checked in the target capabilities tab. That's worth stating explicitly rather than assumed, because an empty entitlements file is a decision, and "just leave whatever Xcode's new-project template generates" is how apps end up with unused capability flags that complicate App Store review for no reason. And on StoreKit testing specifically — since the entitlement/purchase flow is deferred but the seam isn't — I want to lock in now that whoever eventually builds the paid tiers uses a local StoreKit Configuration file for testing in Xcode (simulated transactions, no sandbox App Store account needed), so that when that work happens it's testable in the same "no network dependency" spirit as everything else in this app, rather than requiring a live sandbox tester account to verify. Small thing, but it's exactly the kind of detail that's cheap to decide now and annoying to retrofit later.

Where I stand: everything from my round 1 message holds — UmbraCore as a Foundation-only package, Codable + atomic file writes over SwiftData, zero permissions plus the privacy-manifest call-out, zero third-party dependencies, seeded-PRNG fuzz testing, mutation-function-level testing over XCUITest gesture simulation — plus these two additions, the live-shadow performance acceptance bar and the explicit empty-entitlements/local-StoreKit-testing decision. If Gemma3 or Codex come back with an actual objection to any of that, I'll engage it directly, but absent a real counter-argument on the merits, I think this phase is close to done on substance and I don't want it to stall the way design_discussion did.

**Gemma3 (local) (Frontend Engineer · Visionary) — Round 2**

Okay, let's dive in.

I’m ready to start.

I’m going to focus on building a truly usable and trustworthy experience for this puzzle game. My initial design direction is centered around a single, focused experience – a solitary player manipulating shadows to solve spatial puzzles. The core interaction revolves around *seeing* the effect of the player’s actions, and the game’s success hinges on that visual feedback loop.

Specifically, I’m advocating for a complete avoidance of any layer of abstraction between the player’s direct interaction with the puzzle space and the underlying game state. We’re not building a ‘puzzle game’ – we’re building a spatial reasoning challenge, and the presentation needs to reflect that. That means we need a single, shared canvas view. A single `PuzzleCanvasView` that owns the entire puzzle state – the light source, occluders, target silhouette, and, crucially, the state of the player’s selection. It’s a layer-on-layer design; the canvas is the core, and everything else is simply a visual representation of the chosen manipulation.

I’m calling for a move away from standard iOS list-based navigation for the “Vault” (the level selection screen). It’s a needless abstraction – the player doesn't need a list of *levels*; they need a direct view of the current puzzle. Instead, we present a static thumbnail grid of the current puzzle’s silhouette. Each thumbnail *directly* links to the in-game space. No list, no scrolling – just a clear, immediate view of what the player is tackling.

Furthermore, the core puzzle interaction needs to be *immediate* and tactile. I want to avoid any delay between the player's action (dragging, rotating an occluder) and the visual feedback in the canvas. This means a shared `LevelSessionModel` that centralizes the state—light source position, occlusion state, silhouette position—and reacts instantly to player input, updating the canvas accordingly. This shared model *is* the puzzle space, and it’s the only one we’re dealing with. We are not storing the state in a separate “model” object that the canvas then renders – it's one system.

And finally, the “Solve Confirmation” screen shouldn’t be a separate screen. It’s an in-place confirmation within the puzzle canvas itself. When the player successfully aligns the shadow, the puzzle canvas subtly shifts to show the achieved state – the shadow exactly matching the target. This reinforces the visual feedback loop and avoids the feeling of a ‘level finished’ screen. It's a moment of quiet satisfaction, and it needs to be treated as such.

I’d like to push back on framing this as purely an “iOS puzzle game.” It’s a *spatial reasoning* challenge. The visual presentation has to feel like manipulating a real-world object, not navigating a menu.

I believe this approach prioritizes trust and a satisfying puzzle-solving experience. My biggest concern is ensuring that the Solve Confirmation isn’t just a quick, dismissible moment – it’s a key component of the game’s core appeal. It's a critical design element to avoid the common pitfall of “solving” feeling like just another step in a series of steps; it needs to *feel* like a genuine achievement.

I'm ready to discuss this further and explore the details.

Gemma3 posted essentially the same message as round 1 again — near-verbatim, same canvas/UX architecture pitch — despite being asked directly, twice now, to weigh in on the actual four questions this phase owns: SwiftData vs. Codable, permissions/entitlements, whether zero dependencies really holds, and the test strategy. It never touched any of those. That's the same pattern that closed design_discussion after two rounds of no real engagement, so I'm closing this the same way.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture recommendation:** Split the geometry/solve engine and the canonical level schema into a local Swift Package, UmbraCore, importing only Foundation — no SwiftUI, no UIKit, and Observation kept out of the pure-geometry types. UmbraCore owns the Level/Occluder/LightSource/Target structs, encode/decode, the solve-checker, and the save-time validator. The app target imports UmbraCore and wraps it in the already-locked `@Observable LevelSessionModel` and `VaultStore`. This boundary is what lets the 50-run determinism replay and the fuzz suite run fast and reproducibly via `swift test` with no simulator boot — a slow or flaky suite is one nobody runs before merging, and this is the one architectural risk most likely to quietly undermine the project's core "no silent geometry drift" guarantee if it's skipped.

**Apple framework choices:** SwiftUI for all UI; Observation for the two model types; Foundation for Codable/FileManager; Core Graphics via SwiftUI's `Path`/`Canvas` for shadow rendering, with `CastShadowRenderer` doing closed-form polygon clipping (not sampling/ray-marching) so it stays cheap; CoreHaptics or `UIImpactFeedbackGenerator`/`UINotificationFeedbackGenerator` for the solve haptic; `ShareLink`/`UIActivityViewController` for export; `UIPasteboard.general.string` read only on an explicit user-tapped "paste from clipboard" action, never auto-read on appearance; StoreKit 2 (`Product`, `Transaction`, `Transaction.currentEntitlements`) as a defined-but-unimplemented seam for future paid tiers.

**Persistence and local data plan:** No SwiftData, no CoreData — plain Codable structs, `JSONEncoder`/`JSONDecoder` with an explicit key strategy and a `schemaVersion` field, written to files in Application Support (one vault-manifest file, one file per level, a separate small file for in-progress edit-draft state so a mid-edit crash can't corrupt the vault manifest). All writes are atomic — write to a temp file, then `FileManager.replaceItemAt` — specifically because forced-quit-mid-save is a locked requirement and a half-written file is a real corrupted-save scenario to survive, not just plan around. `UserDefaults` is used only for small flags like the onboarding-seen boolean, never for anything level-shaped.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** Zero runtime permission prompts — no camera, location, contacts, microphone, notifications, or network access, fully offline by construction (no ATS exception needed since there's no networking code at all). Default App Sandbox entitlement only — no App Groups, no iCloud container, no push, no background modes — stated explicitly rather than left as whatever Xcode's template generates. A `PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy` privacy manifest is still required even with zero data collection, called out now as a concrete build task so it doesn't get missed at App Store submission; the actual privacy story is "no data collected," full stop.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party dependencies. Every capability above is first-party. This is stated as a hard decision, not a default, because a convenience package for JSON diffing, geometry, or a share-sheet wrapper would introduce license/supply-chain risk for a problem the app's own geometry code already has to get right itself.

**Testing implications:** The geometry/solve-checker suite lives in the UmbraCore package and uses a custom, explicitly-seeded PRNG (not `SystemRandomNumberGenerator`) so any fuzz failure is deterministically replayable and can be locked in as a new fixture. Serialized-snapshot comparisons use a custom equality check, not synthesized `Equatable` on raw `Double`s, matching the standing two-tier model — epsilon (~1e-8) for live/replay geometry, exact structural equality for canonical serialized snapshots. UI testing deprioritizes XCUITest simulated drags in favor of calling `LevelSessionModel`'s mutation functions directly (the same functions gestures and the discrete VoiceOver controls both route through), plus a thin smoke-level XCUITest pass (launch, open the starter level, confirm the solve sheet appears). New performance acceptance bar, closing an item that's been carried forward unresolved since detailed_discussion: live per-frame shadow recompute must run at 60fps on the oldest iOS-17-supported device at a realistic occluder count, verified with an actual Instruments Time Profiler pass during build — if it's not cheap enough, the fallback is throttling visual recompute to a fixed interval during drag (e.g. 30Hz) while still committing final geometry on gesture-end, never a third-party geometry library. StoreKit testing for the future paid tiers uses a local StoreKit Configuration file (simulated transactions in Xcode), so purchase-flow testing stays no-network-dependency consistent with the rest of the app once that work happens.

**Stall on record:** Two rounds produced no engagement from Codex (CLI unavailable both times) and no new content from Gemma3 beyond a near-verbatim repeat of its design_handoff-era canvas/UX pitch, despite being asked twice, specifically, to address persistence, permissions, dependencies, or testing. This closes on the one substantive architecture proposal actually produced for this phase, consistent with the project's standing instruction to recover cleanly from stalls rather than block indefinitely on non-responsive seats.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback

All six required outputs are present and concrete, not generic boilerplate:

- **SwiftUI architecture**: UmbraCore as a Foundation-only local package (no SwiftUI/UIKit) is a specific, buildable boundary with a clear rationale (fast, reproducible `swift test` runs for the 50-run determinism replay and fuzz suite) tied directly to the project's standing top risk (silent geometry drift).
- **Apple frameworks**: A concrete, first-party-only list mapped to specific responsibilities (Path/Canvas for shadow rendering with an explicit closed-form-clipping constraint, ShareLink/UIActivityViewController, UIPasteboard read gated to explicit user action, StoreKit 2 as a defined seam).
- **Persistence plan**: A specific, justified rejection of SwiftData/CoreData in favor of Codable + atomic file writes, correctly tied back to the locked round-trip-fidelity and forced-quit-recovery requirements rather than asserted as a stylistic preference.
- **Permissions/privacy plan**: Zero runtime permissions, explicit entitlements scope (sandbox only), and a concrete, non-obvious call-out (privacy manifest still required even with no data collection) that reads as genuine domain knowledge rather than filler.
- **Dependency/license decision**: Hard zero-third-party stance with reasoning, satisfying the acceptance check cleanly.
- **Testing implications**: Seeded/replayable PRNG, the epsilon-vs-exact two-tier model correctly carried over from detailed_discussion, mutation-function-level testing over flaky XCUITest gesture simulation, and a new, concrete performance acceptance bar (60fps on oldest supported device, Instruments-verified, with a named non-third-party fallback) that finally closes a risk-register item left dangling across two prior phases.

The stall handling is honest rather than papered over: it names exactly what Codex and Gemma3 did and didn't contribute, doesn't fabricate agreement, and closes on the one substantive proposal actually produced — consistent with how design_discussion was closed. Nothing here contradicts locked upstream decisions (UmbraCore's role matches the canonical-schema requirement from prompt_contract/design_handoff; the entitlements/StoreKit seam matches the deferred-monetization decision from next_steps_small). No ML/AR/LLM use is introduced, which is correctly consistent with the original prompt's "never forced" bonus language — nothing to justify. Ready for tech_specs.

It never touched any of those. That's the same pattern that closed design_discussion after two rounds of no real engagement, so I'm closing this the same way.
